body:
It's not allow to change `buildInfo` during code generation.

After a single build code generation could run multiple times. Modifying it would leave weird side effects.

Instead create a new `Set` and expose that as code generation result. We have a `data` map in the code generation result where this can be places `data.set("topLevelDeclarations", topLevelDeclarations)`

removing that is not correct.

When at least one module doesn't provide topLevelDeclarations we can't tell about the topLevelDeclarations of the concatenated module, so it should be `undefined` (which means "no information")
author: sokra

body:
> And I also think the if (RESERVED_NAMES.has(decl)) continue; is incorrect here. What do you think?

maybe basic idea was that `buildInfo.topLevelDeclarations` are useless in this case since renaming will happen anyway, but yeah this is not correct (will lead to error only in a case of assets. since no populating assets happens on `continue`).
author: vankop

body:
Instead of using both topLevelDeclarations maybe it's better to use

``` js
const topLevelDeclarations =
			(data && data.get("topLevelDeclarations")) ||
			(module.buildInfo && module.buildInfo.topLevelDeclarations);
```

So the topLevelDeclarations from code generations overrides the original topLevelDeclarations, since it's basically a better version of it. It includes renames, which means some original topLevelDeclarations might be no longer in the code generation topLevelDeclarations, e. g. all reserved names.
author: sokra

body:
module context is where nested modules resolves. e.g.
you have files
```
/a/b/c
/a/b/b
```
then request
```js
import(`/a/b/${some}`)
```
will result in context `/a/b` (if no other resolving happen) since all submodules will be resolved related to this context => `./c`, `./b` (`/a/b/c` and `/a/b/b` respectively)
author: vankop

that's right, but the case is different here, because there is module source name mapping, all module generated by webpack which name starts with `webpack://` will get its absolute path to find its module object. see,

body:
sync is not possible for `import()`

and `async-weak` is internal. `weak` will lead to `async-weak` automatically when using `import()`

`lazy-once` is only available for an `import()` with expression.

So this should be `eager | weak | lazy | lazy-once`

And `lazy-once` should be accepted for import() without expression too, and behave as `lazy`

>I think we can add support to any type, we just need to load source map when loadSourceMap is true and pass loaded source map to loader(s)

lets add this to parser options.

> loader can generate additional code and change source map

need @sokra opinion on that.. In this case I think we should keep loader solution. Core should support only source map extraction after loaders run. Since otherwise we still lose source map, if e.g. `babel-loader` will generate another source map.
author: vankop

body:
I also think that we should extract the source map before loaders run.

We can assume (or have to) that loaders handle source map themselves. (They get passed input source map and can return generated source map)

Do we can handle a source map when reading the resource. In NormalModule, where the resource is read.

This might require some changes in loader-runner, since they need to allow returning multiple arguments from `readResource`, or something like that.

We have to think about binary vs string representation of resources. We don't want to do Source Map extraction of binary resources, either do we want to unnecessary convert binary to string. So I guess we want to have a readResource callback where we know if the next loader expects binary or string.

---

(A loader generating a `sourceMappingURL` comment is an incorrect loader, since that's not the way loaders should return a source map. But in that case users can still "fix" the loader by adding the `source-map-loader`, but that's still an exception)

And you should not add dependencies to other modules. So this need to be handled differently.

There is also the problem that a single module might be imported multiple times with different layer, supports and/or media.
author: sokra

body:
So what need to be done here is that `CssImportDependency` need to have `layer` `supports` and `media` properties.
It then need to resolve to different CssModules depending on that. `CssModule` also need to have `layer` `supports` and `media` properties. These need to be inherited to imported module.

CssImportDependency need to override getResourceIdentifier() to add `layer` `supports` and `media`, because they influence resolving and dependencies are grouped based on that identifier.

`CssImportDependency` can't use the `normalModuleFactory` (in CssModulesPlugin). It need to have a custom factory that takes care of copying these properties from the dependency to the module.

I would also omit the decorator dependency at all and take care of adding the decorations in `CssModulePlugin.renderChunk`. Here you can also use the `PrefixSource` to apply indent in a SourceMap compatible way.

body:
I went through this one more time, and it looks good. Just a few questions/highlights that maybe should be answered by @alexander-akait:
- I see that `acorn-import-defer` is an optional peer dependency. When Webpack implemented import attributes, `acorn-import-assertions` was added as a normal dependency, so that the syntax would be available to all users. Should we do something similar here?
- Should we support the comment, even now that we support the syntax? Prettier already supports this syntax, and it will hopefully be included in the next minor release of TypeScript.
- Does webpack have a way to run test262 tests? There are a bunch of them for import defer at https://github.com/tc39/test262/tree/main/test/language/import/import-defer

body:
A [Module Namespace object's defineProperty](https://tc39.es/ecma262/#sec-module-namespace-exotic-objects-defineownproperty-p-desc) checks the new property descriptor. It only returns `true` when the new property descriptor is the same as the current one.

This behaviour is skipped since webpack's implementation (`lib/runtime/MakeNamespaceObjectRuntimeModule.js`) for normal namespace object is skipping this too.

body:
Thank you for your detailed clarification for all comment. ❤️ 

To be honest, if I understand correctly, I believe we don’t need it. We init `module.exports` first and then evaluate the module code. If I’ve misunderstood, could you provide an example of a scenario where removing it would cause issues?

Additionally, we already have some helper runtime functions to ensure CJS module compatibility(i.e. `RuntimeGlobals.createFakeNamespaceObject` for `exportType: default` and `RuntimeGlobals.compatGetDefaultExport` for `exportType: dynamic`), which should be used instead.

Would you mind if I co-authored this PR? It would be great to land it in v5.100.0.
author: hai-x

body:
> We init `module.exports` first and then evaluate the module code.

`module.exports` is initialized when the module first time imported, but this object (`__webpack_module_deferred_exports__[moduleId]`) is required internally (explained above, as the first argument of the Proxy and `module.exports` of that module in the future) when a deferred namespace object of this module is created, at that time, this module may not be imported yet.

> If I’ve misunderstood, could you provide an example of a scenario where removing it would cause issues?

It's possible to implement this feature without `__webpack_module_deferred_exports__`, but that will have a runtime cost that every access to the deferred module namespace object will trigger the Proxy handler. I cannot give an example because it is a runtime optimization.

> Would you mind if I co-authored this PR?

No problem, but I wonder what changes you're proposing.
author: Jack-Works

body:
> but that will have a runtime cost that every access to the deferred module namespace object will trigger the Proxy handler

I might get you now, so  `__webpack_module_deferred_exports__[moduleId]` is used to preserve the original `ns` reference, enabling safe removal of the proxy handler in ESM namespace modules. Without it, reassigning to `ns` would not work.

> but I wonder what changes you're proposing

I think we should use `RuntimeGlobals.createFakeNamespaceObject` and `RuntimeGlobals.compatGetDefaultExport` for CJS compatibility.
author: hai-x

body:
I originally attempted to write a test under asset-modules but could not get it to work, as far as I can see there isn't a mechanism in that test suite to give me the ability to modify an asset between compile steps. I had to heavily tweak the compilation approach in this unit test such that it:
- Used a new `webpack()` instance each time, to avoid relying on shared in-memory state
- Used a physical filesystem, so that cache could be written out and re-used between builds
- Made a deliberate modification to a source file between compile passes

I don't think these three things are possible under `asset-modules`

body:
This implementation looks great. 

We need test cases for at least two/three scenarios: 

- [x] I can use `module.parser.javascript.dynamicImportFetchPriority` and it works correctly w/ high/low/auto
- [x] I can _not_ use `module.parser.javascript.dynamicImportFetchPriority` but instead use magic comments `/* webpackFetchPriority: "high/low/etc." */` and it works
- [x] I can use both features and _override_ at the `import()` level using `webpackFetchPriority:` and it works correctly. 

Here is example test cases you can leverage/learn from: 

* https://github.com/aboktor/webpack/blob/d9b5f321aeb39c394252ad0a7ad109d7e78dec48/test/cases/chunks/inline-options/index.js#L1-L31

* @alexander-akait Is there anywhere else I'm missing?

[

For what it's worth, I know that @aboktor tried setting fetch-priority (just via a quick patch) in an app he was working on, and did not see a statistically-significant end-to-end difference in how quickly that specific app loaded. I have an opportunity to try it out as well in a different context, but won't know if it helped or not for another few weeks.

It still seems like this should only be a good thing; but just an FYI that we don't yet have an empirical-evidence case of where setting the fetch priority helps.

body:
Hi @alexander-akait  ! 
sorry for the delay (work and some holidays). I have push some changes:

- I'm using now RuntimeGlobals.global instead of window, although the error event is only available on window so web workers won't report the error this way, anyway i have taken a look and i would say errors for web workers should be reported in application code as there is no load event (it would be possible to post a message to simlulate load event but this will require to implement some logic around the web worker in webpack's side)
- i have added a test case for this feature and added methods to the global context as current "window"does not have event listeners methods. I must say test execution is really heavy on resources (i executed test script and left to do other things meanwhile and my computer was restarted 🥲 ). To be able to execute them in local i put a limit on workers and concurrency for jest and had some errors regarding timeout and regex  backtracking but those test passed on the current checks so i suppose it is ok, just to know how do you execute all test suites in local?

Let me know if further changes are required
edit: By the way i put the global check because it seems that when compilation is done with output type module there is no __webpack_require__.g being generated on runtime code
author: aespinolopez

body:
Why it is storing as string:
- we can't use `writeBigInt64LE`, because BigInt can be longer than 64bits
- storing by bytes (uint8 or other) will require multiple/shift them in deserialization
- we can use `thing.toString(16)` to reduce size, but I doesn't bring a lot of wins, `4294967296n.toString(16)` -> `100000000`, also it will require to store sing (-/+) and will multiple on -1

so storing them as string, I think, is the best solution
author: alexander-akait

body: Improved, now we bigints as int8 (when `n <= BigInt(127) && n >= BigInt(-128)`) and as int32 (when `n <= BigInt(2147483647) && n >= BigInt(-2147483648)`), other bigints we store as a string with ascii characters, so you can store any bigint

body: Adds a new property called `fallbackVersion`, which is only exposed to `ConsumeSharedPlugin`. It is not directly accessible to `ModuleFederationPlugin` consumers. The value of this property is the truthy value of `version` property passed to `ModuleFederationPlugin`.
author: tmpaul

body: Here, we check if `importResolved` can be mapped out. If there is no resolved import path for a request, we cannot decide whether to exclude it from sharing, so by default `include` it.
author: tmpaul

body: If user has supplied a `version` property to ModuleFed plugin, use that to check against the `exclusionCriteria.version` range
author: tmpaul

body: Find the closest `package.json` for the resolved import. This gives us the version of the package/module that corresponds to the request. Note that there are shared modules that do not have a corresponding `package.json` file, and for these, we do not exclude them from sharing *unless* the user supplies a `version` property. If that is the case, the check in line 269 will activate
author: tmpaul

body: Check that the `package.json` name matches the requested module. Otherwise it can resolve to the root `package.json` of the project.
author: tmpaul

body: Pass in `version` as `fallbackVersion` to exclusionCriteria. As far a consumed module is concerned, the `version` property represents the version of the `fallback`.

body:
exportsWithDashes and exportsWithId are don't need anymore, before exportsWithDashes is used for `var()` to add `--` for its export value, exportsWithId is used for `.locals` to add moduleId before the `.locals`

but now `.locals`, `var()`, and `:export` are all key value pair (`exportName1:value1/exportName2:value2/...`) in `head {}`, `.locals` is represent by `fooBar:./index.css-foo-bar`, `var()` is represent by `varA:--./index.css-var-a`, so they are all key value pair have the same format in `head {}`. In this way it's easier to support `exportsConvention` and `localsIdentName` in the future

Thus, these repeat moduleIds and '--' will no longer be extracted, which make the `head {}` getting bigger

body:
I improved it. Why?

- We don't know target and enviroment 
- But we set `chunkFormat: "module"` only when `output.module` is true and `tp.dynamicImport` is true
- We can load `chunkFormat: "module"` only using `chunkLoading: "import"`
- So let's use `chunkLoading: "import"` when `tp.dynamicImport` or `output.module` is true

So when we can't undestand is `dynamicImport` supported or not and you ask `module` chunk format, we suppose your env supports `import(...)`

Unfortunately, Jest 28 dropped support Node v10. It was mentioned https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/18572#discussion_r1677173885 that Node v10 support should be dropped from webpack, so I figured upgrading the minimum CI test suite to v12 should be uncontroversial.

body:
> I think something wrong with runtimes, because worker always has own runtime

basically, it's due to the `undefined` value of `exportInfo._usedInRuntime`, and this value is only changed by `FlagDependencyUsagePlugin`,  but `this.global` make the plugin pass the deeper modules, so they won't have valid value of  `_usedInRuntime`.

I'm still thinking about how to resolve it, first resolution is to ignore this error, or make the plugin flag deeper modules when project is even configured with usedExport: 'global'

Is there any better resolution?

Yes, it will affect the performance of building. I am open to whether this option is turned on by default. Let me briefly describe the characteristics of this option.

- This optimization does not take effect in all building scenarios. Generally, it usually takes effect when building a library.
- Unfortunately, we have not run any benchmarks here, but what can be determined is that the impact on build performance will grow with the number of entry modules and chunks modules
- It's a trade-off between build efficiency and execution efficiency.

> also why we don't do it when inlinedModules.size > 1

Not fully yet. We checked the through vars in non-inline modules and the module vars in inline modules, and renamed the module var's name there is a collision. That's how the optimization runs now.

But for multiple entries, there also might be a through var in inline module A and a module var in inline module B, which cause the same var leak.

So we can only handle single entry optimization now.

body:
> Can we add a test case with amd, also we support system and other

Sure.

> Do I understand correctly that we are trying to provide the opportunity to use one or another type external depending by dependency category?

The original requirement is that when we build for NPM package, we need to keep `import()` as `import()` and convert `import` to `commonjs` (similar to https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/16272). Considering that we previously added the type of `'module-import'`, supporting map types for `externalsTypes` would be more scalable and more convenient for users. We had thought about exposing some fields of dependency in a function before, but this would expose internal abstractions, so we need to categorize possible categories for users in advance. The finer the category judgment here, the better it can prevent us from having to split existing categories in the future.

> Maybe we can introduce new functions into our dependencies.

I need to review all possible external types again, I previously thought that category covered all enumerations, but now we need to consider the field type.

I added a new `getExternalCategory` to `ModuleDependency`, and some of the subclass implemented it. As of now:

- `AMDRequireItemDependency`: "amd"
- `CommonJsRequireDependency`: "commonjs"
- `HarmonyImportDependency`: "static-import"
- `ImportDependency`: "dynamic-import"

Please take a look, and I think there are more dependencies to add but I'm not sure what they are. Any suggestion please.

body:
I dont think that will work without introducing other problems with eager modules. 

think of this case: 
User shares modules with eager:true, shared modules will only hoist to entrypoint, not runtime - if runtime eagerly share and someone eager require the share - the host runtime will not have the external reference (if the share depends on a external) 

So plugin maybe can be renamed, since its actually HoistEagerExternals. 
In v2, reference hoisting also is responsible for hoisting `@module-federation/runtime,webpack-bundler-runtime,sdk` - since splitChunks will also interfere with federation - this should prevent split chunks from moving eager modules into another chunk beside the runtime.

So while i use it for other things as well, there are cases in federation where it will still fail as anything the system uses or may provide eagerly needs to be in the root chunk at startup, which can be external modules of a shared package as well. 

Id say the only time we dont need to apply it is when there are no seperate `runtimeChunk` added. So like if its just main.js, then webpack already hoists the module to entry chunk. But when there is runtimeChunk, it is only added later in compile, so all modules "hoist" to entry chunk only, not root runtime chunk .

author: timfish

> Is there a reason to not just use a specific (maybe the fastest) hash here?

maybe people want to change the logic depending on the options provided
author: alexander-akait

body:
> the `hashFunction` can now be a constructor to a custom hash function. You can provide a non-crypto hash function for performance reasons.

My understanding is that users can supply a custom `hashFunction` if they want to change the way webpack generates file name hashes.

For Debug IDs we almost certainly don't want users to be able to configure the hash function. According to the spec, Debug ids should be a uuid derived from a deterministic hash of the file source code. The hash type isn't defined in the spec but it should be deterministic and it should have enough entropy that it is effectively unique. If we allow users to configure the hash function, the debug IDs may no longer meet the spec.

In the PR code, the 128bit debug ID is created from two `xxhash64` hashes and on my machine it does this with a 300kB bundle in 2 milliseconds.

body:
I understand your concern.

Fortunately, runtime errors are unlikely to occur because every CSS module imported by a non-CSS file has its exports defined in the webpack_module_cache. And `var module = __webpack_module_cache__[moduleId] = {exports: {}}` ensures that a module object with an empty exports property `{}` is created. As a result, `import foo from "./foo.css"` will still return an empty object ({}), even if we generate an empty source for JS generation of the CSS file.

However, it’s worth noting that these exports will not be namespaced by `webpack/runtime/make namespace object` runtimeModule.

using `const` on this export will create a conflict with `info.name`  on module concatenation!
 https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/d98851529295d2bda276ff892af484bb10da889d/lib/optimize/ConcatenatedModule.js#L1647

This will produce multiple variables declarations with the same name. While using `var` this is not a problem, as soon as `const` is declared this will throw an error in runtime.

```
yarn test:base ./test/ConfigTestCases.basictest.js -t node-worker-esm --no-verbose
nvm i 12.17
node ./test/js/ConfigTestCases/worker/node-worker-esm/bundle.mjs
```

The first generated output code in `test/js` directory, then you will install old node.js version, the third will run code, note you will get on third run - `it("should support ESM worker chunks in Node.js", async () => {`, because there is no jest env, but just open the `./test/js/ConfigTestCases/worker/node-worker-esm/bundle.mjs` and get function in `it` and put at top (sometimes you will need async wrapper) and replace `expect` on `console.log`. It is the faster way to check compatibility.

```
(node:14233) ExperimentalWarning: The ESM module loader is experimental.
(node:14233) ExperimentalWarning: The ESM module loader is experimental.

but it is not a problem, because exit code is `0` and code works fine (and in future versions node.js too).

body:
@xiaoxiaojx  We might want to stick with the current approach.

## What changes with `findNewName`

**Current implementation:**
```javascript
import { a as externals0_a } from "externals0";
import { default as externals2_default } from "externals2";
```

**With `findNewName`:**
```javascript
import { a as _a } from "externals0";
import { default as _default } from "externals2";
```

## The trade-offs

While `findNewName` does give us webpack's standard naming utility and better reserved word handling, we lose some important context:

- `externals0_a` → `_a` makes it harder to track which module imports come from
- Debugging becomes trickier when you can't immediately see the source


## My take

Since this PR is focused on getting ESM tree-shaking working, I'd suggest keeping the current naming implementation. We can always revisit the `findNewName` migration in a follow-up PR where we can properly address the test updates and discuss the DX implications.

body:
Right, while I initially thought of these as "Worker options" (placing them semantically with the options object), the `parseCommentOptions` function actually scans the entire expression range, making both patterns work seamlessly.

So, us can place their webpack magic comments in either location:
- **Before the URL** (webpack's standard pattern) ✅
  ```js
  new Worker(/* webpackChunkName: "worker" */ './worker.js')
  ```
- **Inside the options object** (backward compatible) ✅
  ```js
  new Worker('./worker.js', {
    /* webpackChunkName: "worker" */
  })
  ```

I've added tests to ensure both patterns continue to work properly. 08a115f92d49bc309e53fabee208fbc4c8ce270a

body:
@alexander-akait You're right. I understand the issue now.

## Implementation Plan

### Step 1: Implement `new URL()` prefetch/preload support first

As you suggested, this is the more general solution that benefits all URL-based modules:

```javascript
new URL(
  /* webpackPrefetch: true */
  /* webpackPreload: 3 */
  "./heavy-asset.wasm",
  import.meta.url
)
```


### Step 2: Add Worker integration on top of URL support

Once `new URL()` supports prefetch/preload, Worker support becomes straightforward:

```javascript
new Worker(
  /* webpackPrefetch: true */
  new URL("./worker.js", import.meta.url)
)
```

- Emit warnings when both are specified
- Maintain backward compatibility

### Step 3: Handle edge cases and conflicts

```javascript
// Conflict resolution
new Worker(
  /* webpackPrefetch: 1 */  // This wins
  new URL(
    /* webpackPrefetch: 2 */ // This is ignored with warning
    "./worker.js",
    import.meta.url
  )
)
```

### Addressing the timing concern (before/after chunk loading)

For the timing issue that blocked #18524, I propose adding an explicit control:

```javascript
new URL(
  /* webpackPrefetch: true */
  /* webpackPrefetchTiming: "immediate" | "afterLoad" | "onIdle" */
  "./asset.wasm",
  import.meta.url
)
```

- **immediate**: Start prefetching as soon as the parent chunk loads (before)
- **afterLoad**: Wait until the URL is actually used once (after)
- **onIdle**: Use requestIdleCallback (default behavior)

Would this approach address the timing concerns?
author: 3ru

body:
I think we don't need syntax for `new Worker` at all, only for `new URL`, because it has not sense,

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Reference/Attributes/rel/prefetch comments for prefetch

Shorty - we should emulate HTML `link[prefetch]` and `link[preload]` behavior here

About when we should load it - it is good question, I think loading them after loading chunk by default is good place todo it, let's wait an answer from @hai-x @xiaoxiaojx 

Anyway you can start to implement it after chunk loading, it is not hard to change it or implement behavior `webpackPrefetchTiming`, need a discussion here.

Also feel free to reuse my work from linked PR
author: alexander-akait

body:
After trial and error, I've successfully implemented the feature for `new URL()` syntax as you suggested:

```javascript
new URL(
	/* webpackPrefetch: true */
	/* webpackPreload: true */
	/* webpackFetchPriority: "high" */
	"./asset.png",
	import.meta.url
)
```

This approach is indeed more general and useful - it works for any `new URL()` modules including assets, and naturally extends to Worker usage:

```ts
new Worker(
	new URL(/* webpackPrefetch: true */ "./worker.js", import.meta.url)
)
```

I realize now our initial discussion had some crossed wires about the implementation approach.

body:
Let's add `dotenv : boolean | DotenvPluginOptions` option at top of webpack.config.js, i.e. when you have 

**webpack.config.js**

```js
export default {
  mode: "development",
  dotenv: true
}
```

we will apply this plugin by default (also this option should be exposed to CLI), i.e. `webpack --dotenv` should enable this plugin
author: alexander-akait

body: I think we don't need this option, let's always expand variables, almost all developers use it...
author: alexander-akait

body: Should we set it `true` by default? Can you look at nextjs/vite, how they work here?
author: alexander-akait

body: We should allow to use multiple paths here and use the same value here - https://vite.dev/guide/env-and-mode.html#env-files (`[mode]` allowed placeholder and replaced by current mode)
author: alexander-akait

body: Let's expose only `WEBPACK_` variables by default like vite does too https://vite.dev/guide/env-and-mode.html#env-variables (we need an extra option for this to setup this value, can be array, i.e. ["WEBPACK", "CUSTOM"])
author: alexander-akait

body: Do we really need this? Can you find useful case for this options? I think multiple `paths` can solve this problem
author: alexander-akait

body: I think we will need an option for this, some developers can use non standard filenames (I saw it many times), so what about `template` option? It will be array, like `template: [".env", ".env.local", ".env.[mode]", ".env.[mode].local"]`, only `[mode]` placeholder supported
author: alexander-akait

body: Looks very good, let's make one improvement and we can merge
author: alexander-akait

body: Place the plugin under `webpack.experiments.ManifestPlugin`. Any better suggestions?
author: hai-x

body: Let's make this non `experiments`, I don't think we will have a lot of changes for this plugin in future
author: alexander-akait
alexander-akait

body: Let's use `{ ... }` (spread)
author: alexander-akait

body: Let's use a WeakMap here - `WeakMap<Compilation, Map<string, Module>>`, so we don't need to clear this because no compilation - no map
author: alexander-akait

body: Make sense,  thank you.
author: hai-x

body: We can cache it using WeakMap too - `WeakMap<Compilation, JSONValue>`, maybe we can union two caches above into one, i.e. `WeakMap<Compilation, { assets: Map<string, Module>, jsonStats: JSONValue }>`
author: alexander-akait

body: Also let's implement file-system (and memory cache here) using our cache API, using `compilation.getCache` and `cache.getItemCache`, look at https://github.com/webpack-contrib/terser-webpack-plugin/blob/master/src/index.js for example
author: alexander-akait

body: Let's add a simple `example` too
author: alexander-akait

body: Let's avoid it, don't know why we need resolve than relative, we always emit files to `(compiler.options).output.path`, so use `this.options.filename` directly in `compilation.emitAsset`
author: alexander-akait

body: After reconsidering, I think the manifest generation logic won’t add much overhead. If we do decide to cache it, we should use hashing etags for all assets—but that could itself introduce some hashing overhead. What do you think?
author: hai-x

body: I refactor it and now we only need `WeakMap<Compilation, Stats>`.
author: hai-x

body: I refactor it and we can don't rely on it now. There are some old logic in `webpack-manifest-plugin`. Now we have already supported `sourceFilename` in `loaderContext.emitFile` for more DX friendly.
author: hai-x

body: After discussing, we don't need to consider/support absolute filename (we have options' schema validation for absolute filename). So i remove those logic now.
author: hai-x

body: I’ve updated it to `handle(manifest: Record<string, string>, stats: Stats) -> string`. This allows users to define more custom logic (e.g., outputting in YAML format).
author: hai-x

body: When we need to use regexp except `html-webpack-plugin`? Only for entries/chunks? Because for assets we use `sourceFilename`, maybe we can emit for entries/chunks - `originalModuleFilename` property in asset info and use it here to avoid using hashes?
author: alexander-akait

body: To be honest, i think it's only for `html-webpack-plugin`. For `entries/chunks`, user can have `contenthash: 8` and it won't work for them.
author: hai-x

body: ## Pull Request Overview This PR documents the webpack core working group by adding a formal charter and updating the README to reference it. The changes establish governance structure and responsibilities for the core development team. - Adds a comprehensive working group charter document outlining purpose, responsibilities, and membership - Updates README to replace the Core Collaborators section with a reference to the new working group - Transitions from informal "core" team structure to formal "core working group" governance model ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 4 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | WORKING_GROUP.md | New charter document defining the core working group's purpose, responsibilities, members, and communication protocols | | README.md | Updated to remove Core Collaborators section and reference the new working group charter | --- **Tip:** Customize your code reviews with copilot-instructions.md. Create the file or learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
There are two spelling errors: 'workign' should be 'working' and 'grpup' should be 'group'.
```suggestion
Each member of the working group is required to adhere to the [governance model of webpack](https://github.com/webpack/governance). In addition to having responsibility of the main part of webpack, the working group will manage other parts of the webpack organization repositories in such way they see fit.
```

The purpose of this working group is to facilitate, implement and fix problems and features within the core of webpack. This core includes tasks such as bugfixes, feature implementation and in general maintaining the main logic of webpack.

The phrase should be 'on an as-needed basis' instead of 'on-need basis', and 'a appropriate' should be 'an appropriate'.

Meetings are hosted on an as-needed basis and private discussions are held in the #core-wg channel. For public feedback and communication, please use an appropriate channel within the webpack discord (e.g `#development-general`).

body: ## Pull Request Overview This PR refactors the benchmark testing infrastructure by consolidating timing logic and improving code organization. The changes primarily move timing measurements (`console.time`/`console.timeEnd`) from individual test cases into reusable wrapper functions and enable debug mode for CodSpeed benchmarking. Key changes: - Extracted timing logic into `fnWrapper` functions within `runTaskAsync` and `runTaskSync` - Moved function definitions to the top of their respective scopes for better readability - Removed redundant timing calls from individual benchmark test cases - Enabled CodSpeed debug mode in the CI workflow ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | test/BenchmarkTestCases.benchmark.mjs | Refactored timing measurement logic into reusable wrapper functions and reorganized function definitions | | .github/workflows/test.yml | Added CODSPEED_DEBUG environment variable to enable debug mode | --- **Tip:** Customize your code reviews with copilot-instructions.md. body:
Using `EnvironmentPlugin` is a breaking change (and can lead to unexpected behavior), we should use DefinePlugin here, because EnvironmentPlugin has

```js
const value =
				process.env[key] !== undefined
					? process.env[key]
					: this.defaultValues[key];
```

So if you have `process.env.NODE_ENV` in your `process.env` we will override the value

I think we should use DefinePlugin in Dotenv plugin too, because
author: alexander-akait

I’m not sure if we really need an ImportMetaEnvPlugin. It seems sufficient to simply add import.meta.env.xxx keys for all process.env.xxx variables.

author: JSerFeng

body:
It seems that whether `redirect-warn` or `redirect-error` is used depends on whether the current module is `strict ESM` — or, more precisely, on its `exportPresence`. In this case, named import JSON module in `strict ESM` will always cause a linking error (not a warning).

So if we really want to align with `esbuild` and support named exports/imports, I think we could avoid throwing the linking error. And if we do that, how about simply reusing redirect to keep the logic straightforward and avoid extra complexity?
author: hai-x

body:
The behavior in this PR is that:

named import always success for normal import (without attributes). **But** there is always a warning, not error.

named import always fail for import with attributes.

Do you mean we just do nothing but redirect the exports, and doesn't warn user @hai-x
author: JSerFeng

body:
> there is always a warning, not an error.

To be honest, I think whether it’s a `warning` or an `error` depends on whether the current module is a `strict ESM` — or, more precisely, on its `exportPresence`. You can enable `module.strictExportPresence: true `to verify this behavior, and it will throw an error and cause the compilation to fail.

> named import always fails for import with attributes.

I agree with you — we should set it to false and use `default-only` in this case.

> Do you mean we just do nothing but redirect the exports, and doesn't warn user

Yeah, i think we can allow named export/import if we want to align with `esbuild`. I'm fine with it.
author: hai-x

body:
> To be honest, I think whether it’s a warning or an error depends on whether the current module is a strict ESM — or, more precisely, on its exportPresence. You can enable module.strictExportPresence: true to verify this behavior, and it will throw an error and cause the compilation to fail.

Agree
author: alexander-akait

body: I get it
author: JSerFeng

body:
@JSerFeng After a small internal discussion we decided to keep current behavior when  `namedExports` is `undefined`, i.e.:

- When `namedExports` is `undefined` - we use `redirect-warn` (because using named export for JSON is not recommended at all)
- When `namedExports` is `true`, you can use it without errors (for old/legacy code where named export was used)
- When `namedExports` is `false` we throw error and you can't use named export

Also `namedExports` will work only when you have an object in JSON file

body:
Currently I think it's the most safe way, I choose EsmSideEffectDependency because it just means this connection is for side effect, and we can ensure the js output of CssModule has no side effect(when disabled hmr). so this connection can be safely removed.

But others like require, user can write like this

```js
const o = require('./foo.css')

call(o)
```

There is only one connection, and it contains CjsRequireDependency. CjsRequireDependency is not like EsmSideEffectDependency, it can have side effect and likely to consume the result. We cannot safely remove this connection.
author: JSerFeng

body: What about just `require('./foo.css')`? Technically we can remove empty JS modules here too
author: alexander-akait

body: Yes, but we can't do the same for `require` using the solution of this PR `SideEffectDependency` means connection and executing side effect, we can know css has no side effect so we can remove `SideEffectDependency`. If exports is used, there is `SideEffectDependency` and `SpecifierDependency`, so we can know it's exports are used If exports is not used, there is only `SideEffectDependency`, so we can know it's exports are not used But `RequireDependency` means connection and has access the exports, we can't know if the importer uses exports or not. Whether exports are used or not can not be told just by dependencies. You can see there is no difference.
author: JSerFeng

body:
## Pull request overview This PR adds Changesets automation for managing releases in the webpack repository. It introduces the `@changesets/cli` package as a dev dependency, configures changesets with a config file, and creates a GitHub Actions workflow to automate the release process including creating release PRs and publishing to npm. - Adds `@changesets/cli@^2.29.8` as a dev dependency - Creates `.changeset/config.json` with changeset configuration - Introduces `.github/workflows/release.yml` workflow for automated releases - Updates examples workflow to include `fetch-tags: true` ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 5 out of 6 changed files in this pull request and generated 4 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | package.json | Adds @changesets/cli as a dev dependency to enable changeset commands | | yarn.lock | Adds all dependencies required by changesets and its transitive dependencies | | .changeset/config.json | Configures changeset behavior including changelog format, access level, and base branch | | .changeset/README.md | Provides documentation about the changesets tool for contributors | | .github/workflows/release.yml | Defines the automated release workflow that runs on pushes to main | | .github/workflows/examples.yml | Adds fetch-tags option to ensure tags are available during checkout |

body:
The `actions/checkout` action is using version `v3`, which is inconsistent with other workflows in this repository that use the pinned commit hash `1af3b93b6815bc44a9784bd300feb67ff0d1eeb3 # v6.0.0`. 

For security and consistency, consider using the same version as other workflows:
```yaml
uses: actions/checkout@1af3b93b6815bc44a9784bd300feb67ff0d1eeb3 # v6.0.0
```
```suggestion
        uses: actions/checkout@1af3b93b6815bc44a9784bd300feb67ff0d1eeb3 # v6.0.0
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The changeset config has `"access": "restricted"`, but the main `package.json` does not have `"private": true`. For a public npm package like webpack, the access should be `"public"` instead of `"restricted"`.

Consider changing:
```json
"access": "public",
```
```suggestion
  "access": "public",
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The checkout action may need `fetch-depth: 0` to fetch all git history for changesets to properly determine version bumps based on conventional commits and changeset files. Without full git history, changesets might not work correctly.

Consider adding:
```yaml
- name: Checkout Repo
  uses: actions/checkout@v3
  with:
    fetch-depth: 0
```
```suggestion
        uses: actions/checkout@v3
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The release workflow is missing explicit `permissions` configuration. Following security best practices evident in other workflows in this repository, permissions should be explicitly declared to follow the principle of least privilege.

Since this workflow needs to create pull requests and potentially publish to npm, consider adding:
```yaml
permissions:
  contents: write
  pull-requests: write
```
```suggestion

permissions:
  contents: write
  pull-requests: write
```

body:
The checkout action is missing `fetch-depth: 0` which is important for changesets to work correctly. Changesets needs access to the full git history to determine which packages have changed and generate proper changelogs. Add this configuration:
```yaml
- uses: actions/checkout@1af3b93b6815bc44a9784bd300feb67ff0d1eeb3 # v6.0.0
  with:
    fetch-depth: 0
```
```suggestion
      - uses: actions/checkout@1af3b93b6815bc44a9784bd300feb67ff0d1eeb3 # v6.0.0
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The workflow is missing the `publish` input parameter. The changesets action will attempt to publish to npm by default when an NPM_TOKEN is provided. Since `NPM_TOKEN` is set to an empty string, you should explicitly disable publishing by adding:
```yaml
with:
  commit: "[ci] release"
  title: "[ci] release"
  publish: false
```
This will prevent the action from attempting npm publish operations.
```suggestion
          title: "[ci] release"
          publish: false
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The workflow is missing `corepack enable` which is needed to ensure yarn is available and using the correct version specified in `package.json` (`yarn@1.22.22`). Other workflows in this repository include this step. Add it before the Node.js setup:
```yaml
- uses: actions/checkout@1af3b93b6815bc44a9784bd300feb67ff0d1eeb3 # v6.0.0
- run: corepack enable
- name: Use Node.js
```
```suggestion

      - run: corepack enable
```

body: ## Pull request overview This PR fixes a regression introduced in #20268 where ConsumeSharedModule fails when module concatenation optimization is enabled. The issue occurs because ConcatenatedModule wraps the actual module, and moduleGraph stores export information for the wrapped rootModule rather than the ConcatenatedModule itself. **Changes:** - Modified `ConsumeSharedModule.getExportsType()` to extract and use `rootModule` from `ConcatenatedModule` before calling `getExportsType()` --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

body:
## Pull request overview This PR introduces experimental support for TC39 source-phase imports for WebAssembly modules in webpack, allowing `import.source()` of `.wasm`/`.wat` to yield a `WebAssembly.Module` object instead of an instantiated instance. It wires the feature through parser options, experiments config, runtime globals, wasm async loading/compilation, and adds tests and schema/type updates. **Changes:** - Add `experiments.importSource` and associated JavaScript parser options (`parser.javascript*.importSource`) to configuration schemas, defaults, CLI snapshots, and TypeScript declaration files. - Extend the import phase infrastructure to support a new `Source` phase, integrate it with both ESM and dynamic import parsers, and plumb a `sourcePhase` flag through `NormalModule` and the async WebAssembly module pipeline. - Introduce `RuntimeGlobals.compileWasm` and a new `AsyncWasmCompileRuntimeModule`, along with generator/parser changes so async WebAssembly modules imported via `import.source()` compile to and export a cached `WebAssembly.Module` default, plus config-case tests for basic behavior and caching. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 23 out of 26 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `types.d.ts` | Adds `experiments.importSource`, parser `importSource` options, `ImportExpressionJavascriptParser.phase` support for `"source"`, `NormalModule.sourcePhase`, and `RuntimeGlobals.compileWasm` to the public type surface. | | `declarations/WebpackOptions.d.ts` | Mirrors new `importSource` experiment and parser options in the exported TS configuration interfaces. | | `schemas/WebpackOptions.json` | Extends the JSON schema with `experiments.importSource` and `module.parser.javascript*.importSource` boolean options so config validation/CLI help know about the new feature. | | `lib/config/defaults.js` | Wires `experiments.importSource` into defaults, passes it through module/parser option defaults, and ensures JS parser `importSource` defaults from the experiment flag. | | `lib/WebpackOptionsApply.js` | Enables `acorn-import-phases` whenever `deferImport` or `importSource` is enabled and configures it to recognize `source`-phase syntax when `experiments.importSource` is true. | | `lib/javascript/JavascriptParser.js` | Updates the `ImportExpression` typedef so the AST node can carry `phase: "defer" | "source"` from the parser. | | `lib/dependencies/ImportPhase.js` | Introduces `ImportPhase.Source` and `ImportPhaseUtils.isSource`, and updates `createGetImportPhase` to classify both `defer` and `source` syntax when enabled. | | `lib/dependencies/ImportParserPlugin.js` | Switches import-phase detection to be gated by either `deferImport` or `importSource` parser options for dynamic imports. | | `lib/dependencies/HarmonyImportDependencyParserPlugin.js` | Same as above for static `import`/`export` (harmony) parsing. | | `lib/dependencies/ImportDependency.js` | Uses `ImportPhaseUtils` and, for source-phase imports, unwraps the default export so `import.source()` resolves to the underlying value (the `WebAssembly.Module`) instead of the namespace object. | | `lib/dependencies/HarmonyImportDependency.js` | Extends debugging/serialization output to reflect `|source` phase, skips export linking checks for source-phase imports, and adjusts the internal key to distinguish `"source"` imports. | | `lib/RuntimeGlobals.js` | Registers a new runtime global `compileWasm` (`__webpack_require__.vs`) with documentation indicating it compiles a wasm module from id and hash to `WebAssembly.Module`. | | `lib/WebpackOptionsApply.js` | Ensures that when `experiments.importSource` is enabled, the parser is extended with `source`-phase import support, without affecting builds where the experiment is off. | | `lib/NormalModule.js` | Adds a `sourcePhase` flag to `NormalModule` construction data, stores it on instances, and includes it in serialization/deserialization for caching. | | `lib/wasm-async/AsyncWebAssemblyModulesPlugin.js` | Detects when an async wasm module is reached via a source-phase dependency and marks its create data as `sourcePhase` so downstream parser/generator behavior can switch. | | `lib/wasm-async/AsyncWebAssemblyParser.js` | For `NormalModule.sourcePhase` wasm modules, validates only the wasm magic header, marks `buildMeta.sourcePhase`, declares a single default export, and skips full wasm decoding; otherwise keeps existing async wasm parsing. | | `lib/wasm-async/AsyncWebAssemblyJavascriptGenerator.js` | Routes source generation for `buildMeta.sourcePhase` modules to a new `_generateSourcePhase` code path that defines an async module using `RuntimeGlobals.compileWasm` and exports the resulting `WebAssembly.Module` as `default`. | | `lib/wasm-async/AsyncWasmCompileRuntimeModule.js` | New runtime module that defines `RuntimeGlobals.compileWasm` as a function taking `(wasmModuleId, wasmModuleHash)`, loading the wasm asset (via target-specific loader code), using `WebAssembly.compile` / `compileStreaming` with a MIME-type-sensitive fallback, and returning a `Promise`. | | `lib/web/FetchCompileAsyncWasmPlugin.js` | Hooks the web/`fetch` wasm loading pipeline to also provide the new compile runtime using `AsyncWasmCompileRuntimeModule` when async wasm modules are present, sharing the same `generateLoadBinaryCode` as instantiation. | | `lib/node/ReadFileCompileAsyncWasmPlugin.js` | Similarly wires the node/`async-node` wasm pipeline to add `AsyncWasmCompileRuntimeModule` (non-streaming, using fs/url loader) when async wasm modules are in the chunk. | | `test/configCases/wasm/source-phase-basic/webpack.config.js` | Adds an async-node test configuration enabling `experiments.importSource` and async wasm via a `.wat` loader. | | `test/configCases/wasm/source-phase-basic/wasm.wat` | Simple WAT module exporting an `add(i32, i32)` function to be used in source-phase import tests. | | `test/configCases/wasm/source-phase-basic/test.filter.js` | Skips the new wasm test config when the environment lacks WebAssembly support. | | `test/configCases/wasm/source-phase-basic/index.js` | Test cases verifying `import.source("./wasm.wat")` yields a `WebAssembly.Module`, that it can be instantiated and invoked, and that multiple imports share the same compiled module instance. | | `test/__snapshots__/Cli.basictest.js.snap` | Updates CLI option snapshots to include `experiments.importSource` and all `module.parser.javascript*.importSource` flags with consistent descriptions. | | `test/Defaults.unittest.js` | Adjusts defaults snapshots to reflect `experiments.importSource: false` and parser `importSource: false` by default. |

body:
This is an example of using [Conditional Types](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/conditional-types.html) in TypeScript; the way I interpret it initially, it's a way to declare generic types that can be implemented for a variety of possibly-disjoint -- but always well-typed -- datastructures.

As far as I can tell, the top-level type being declared here (the one that has this multi-nested chain of conditional types associated with it) -- `StatsObject` -- is only used in a single file within the source code at the moment, in some comment-based API documentation: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/26e549ab989a6e68c108f4efa6ffe502b787a92b/lib/stats/StatsFactory.js#L63-L75

body: ## Pull request overview This PR fixes a crash in webpack when using Web Workers with transpiled code (e.g., via babel-loader). The crash occurred during the hash creation phase when webpack tried to build a dependency graph of runtime chunks but encountered worker chunks that don't have runtime. **Changes:** - Added a guard to check if `otherInfo` is defined before accessing its properties when building the runtime chunk dependency graph - Added an explanatory comment documenting why the guard is necessary --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Also please add a test case
author: alexander-akait

body:
## Pull request overview This PR fixes a bug where only the last entry module's exports were exposed when using `output.library` with multiple entry modules (e.g., `entry: ["a", "b", "c"]`). The fix ensures that all entry modules' exports are aggregated into the library object by: (1) calling `finishEntryModule` for all entry dependencies in AbstractLibraryPlugin, not just the last one, and (2) passing the same shared exports object to all entry modules in JavascriptModulesPlugin's startup code generation. **Changes:** - AbstractLibraryPlugin now processes all entry dependencies for library export marking - JavascriptModulesPlugin's bootstrap path shares exports object across all entry modules when library output is configured - JavascriptModulesPlugin's inlined path adds logic to merge exports from multiple entry modules - Test case added to verify the fix with three entry modules exporting different constants ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 7 out of 7 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | lib/library/AbstractLibraryPlugin.js | Changed to iterate through all entry dependencies and call `finishEntryModule` for each, instead of only processing the last dependency | | lib/javascript/JavascriptModulesPlugin.js | Added `mergeLibraryEntryExports` flag and logic to share `RuntimeGlobals.exports` across all entry modules in both inlined and bootstrap code paths | | test/configCases/library/multiple-entry-exports-15936/webpack.config.js | Test configuration with three entry modules and assign library type | | test/configCases/library/multiple-entry-exports-15936/test.config.js | Test cleanup to remove global library object | | test/configCases/library/multiple-entry-exports-15936/a.js | First entry module exporting constant `a` | | test/configCases/library/multiple-entry-exports-15936/b.js | Second entry module exporting constant `b` | | test/configCases/library/multiple-entry-exports-15936/c.js | Third entry module exporting constant `c` and containing test assertions |
--- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
When multiple entry modules are inlined with `mergeLibraryEntryExports`, the existing logic at lines 1020-1025 (not shown in diff) wraps each entry module in an IIFE for isolation. The export variable declarations at lines 1070-1073 are then placed inside these IIFE scopes.

For ES modules that use property assignments (e.g., `__webpack_exports__.a = 1`), this works because the outer `__webpack_exports__` is accessed through scope chain lookup. However, if a module reassigns its exports variable (e.g., `exports = {...}`), the connection to the shared `__webpack_exports__` would be broken, preventing export aggregation.

While ES modules typically only do property assignments, this creates a fragile dependency on module code behavior. Consider explicitly preventing IIFE wrapping when `mergeLibraryEntryExports` is true, or documenting this limitation.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

This avoids requiring users to configure the context option and also prevents configs from being cluttered with repeated `"auto"` values, like `{ mod1: { context: "auto", source() {} }, mod2: { context: "auto", source() {} }, mod3: { context: "auto", source() {} } }`.
author: xiaoxiaojx

body:
I am afraid that it is a breaking change, `auto` is the new behavior, old virtual modules can be broken, right?

Do I understand correctly this feature, if I have `/path/to/src/components/button.js`, context will be `path/to/src/components/`?
author: alexander-akait

body:
This won’t affect existing virtual modules. Previously, virtual module paths like `"virtual:routes"` were only used as unique IDs, and their context was always fixed to compiler.context. With `context: "auto"`, for `"virtual:routes" `the computed context is still compiler.context, so nothing changes.

Now we’d like to extend virtual module paths so they’re not just IDs, but virtual paths with context information — for example, `virtual:src/components/button.js`. In that case, the context would be resolved to something like `/path/to/src/components/button.js`.

body:
Good eye, but this is actually consistent with webpack's existing hash convention. The `WasmHash` implementation (used by both `xxhash64` and `md4`) does the same thing in `lib/util/hash/wasm-hash.js:201`:

```js
if (encoding === "binary" || !encoding) return Buffer.from(hex, "hex");
```

In webpack's hash interface, `"binary"` means "return raw binary data as a Buffer", rather than the Node.js `latin1` alias. This convention is established across all the built-in hash implementations.
author: apoorvdarshan

body: In `dynamic-reexport`, the new logic still always returns `Dependency.EXPORTS_OBJECT_REFERENCED` when the imported module exists because `importedExportsInfo.otherExportsInfo.provided !== false` is a precondition for reaching `mode.type === "dynamic-reexport"` (see `getStarReexports`/`getMode`). That makes the computed `referencedExports` effectively unused and prevents the intended tree-shaking improvement. Consider returning the enumerated export names here (and only falling back to `EXPORTS_OBJECT_REFERENCED` when the mode can actually occur with unknown exports), or refactor the mode conditions so this branch can be reached when `otherExportsInfo.provided === false`.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The runtime code for `dynamic-reexport` filters out both `mode.ignored` and `mode.hidden` exports (it combines them before the `for..in`), but `getReferencedExports` only skips `mode.ignored`. This can incorrectly mark hidden exports as referenced/used and reduce tree-shaking precision. The referenced export list should apply the same combined ignore set as the generated runtime reexport loop.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `referencedExports` is an untyped empty array here, while other branches in this switch annotate it as `/** @type {RawReferencedExports} */` before passing it around. Adding the JSDoc type annotation would keep the file consistent and helps static analysis tooling catch shape regressions.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: This change is intended to improve tree-shaking for `dynamic-reexport`, but there’s no accompanying regression test exercising the `mode.type === "dynamic-reexport"` path and asserting that an imported module’s unused `default` export is not marked as used. Adding a focused test (likely near existing `test/cases/side-effects/dynamic-reexports/*`) would prevent regressions and ensure the optimization is actually applied.

(slow reply, it took me a while to figure out running a small subset of test cases, despite the helpful [documentation](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/724c209d2fd0b3432bb3e681cb51547a9591b4a5/TESTING_DOCS.md?plain=1#L129)!)

I think it's better to leave the empty-array-export from `warnings.js` in place here.

...because the `checkArrayExpectation` function used to [inspect test result warnings](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/724c209d2fd0b3432bb3e681cb51547a9591b4a5/test/TestCases.template.js#L396-L407) reports a problem when [more warnings than expected](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/724c209d2fd0b3432bb3e681cb51547a9591b4a5/test/checkArrayExpectation.js#L95) are emitted -- and that logic exists within a code block that only runs when the `warnings.js` file exists: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/724c209d2fd0b3432bb3e681cb51547a9591b4a5/test/checkArrayExpectation.js#L86

So, if we retain the export-array-export, then compilation warnings will fail the test case, improving the chance that we'll investigate and fix whatever introduced that.

Or, to phrase that conversely: if we removed the `warnings.js` file here, then compile-time warnings could occur for the `asi` and `import-meta` test cases, and the test suite wouldn't detect them.

So: I've no plans to open a pull request after all.  Apologies for the distraction!

body: ## Pull request overview Fixes a webpack UMD + `experiments.lazyCompilation` + first HMR update edge case where externals referenced only from the first lazily-loaded module can be evaluated outside the UMD wrapper scope and fail to resolve (issue #19134). **Changes:** - Update UMD/UMD2 external codegen to resolve externals via wrapper-local binding when available, otherwise via a runtime-global cached value, otherwise via the real global external. - Register the resolved UMD external into a runtime-global slot so subsequent hot updates can reuse it. - Add an integration test + fixtures that build a UMD bundle with lazy compilation, trigger a lazy import, rebuild, apply the first hot update, and assert the external-backed lazy module resolves on first load. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 4 out of 4 changed files in this pull request and generated 4 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | lib/ExternalModule.js | Adjusts UMD/UMD2 external source generation to recover externals across lazy-compilation hot update scope boundaries via a global cache. | | test/LazyCompilationUmdExternals.test.js | Adds an integration test covering lazyCompilation + UMD externals + first HMR update. | | test/fixtures/lazy-compilation-umd-externals/entry.js | Fixture entry that triggers the lazy import and applies the first HMR update. | | test/fixtures/lazy-compilation-umd-externals/module.js | Fixture lazy module that imports an external (with fake-update-loader variant support). | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

body:
This fixture has two fake-update-loader variants separated by `---`, but both variants are identical. Since `fake-update-loader` selects the variant by `updateIndex`, recompiling after incrementing `updateIndex` will likely produce no changed output and therefore no HMR update (causing `module.hot.check(true)` to return `null`/no update). Make the second variant differ (while still exporting the external) so the second compilation reliably emits an update.
```suggestion

export const updated = true;
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `getSourceForUmdExternal` no longer incorporates the `this.isOptional(moduleGraph)` behavior that other external types use via `checkExternalVariable(...)`. For optional externals (e.g. in a try/catch optional dependency context), this changes runtime semantics by returning `undefined` instead of throwing a missing-module error. Consider threading the `optional` flag into `getSourceForUmdExternal` and emitting a `throwMissingModuleErrorBlock` when the resolved external value is still `undefined`.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
copilot-pull-request-reviewer
copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Splitting AMD/UMD handling so `getSourceForUmdExternal` registers the binding on `runtimeTemplate.globalObject` before falling back to `getSourceForGlobalVariableExternal` matches how lazy chunks hydrate externals on first load. The `LazyCompilationUmdExternals` fixture + HMR round-trip is a solid repro.

nit: `setTimeout(resolve, 1000)` in the test can flake on slow CI runners; a hook on script load or polling the output might be tighter.

nit: `__webpack_external_value__` is a single name in the generated init; if multiple externals ever share that scope without an IIFE, name collision risk is there (might already be impossible for this code path).

## Pull request overview Adds experimental TypeScript handling to the JavaScript pipeline by enabling a TypeScript transform/strip step (via Node’s TypeScript stripping API), wiring defaults/schema/types for opt-in configuration, and introducing config-case coverage for key TS resolution/parsing scenarios. **Changes:** - Add `experiments.typescript` and `module.parser.javascript*.typescript` options to schemas and TypeScript declaration files. - Enable TypeScript-aware defaults (module rules for `.ts/.mts/.cts`, `resolve.extensionAlias`, `resolve.tsconfig`, and `.ts` resolution ordering under the experiment / future defaults). - Add extensive config-case tests for TS features (type-only imports, namespaces, CJS `require`, mts/cts, data-uri, imports field, dynamic import, etc.) and update CLI/default snapshots. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 81 out of 84 changed files in this pull request and generated 7 comments.

body: `module.getResource()` can include a query string (e.g. `file.ts?something`). The current `\.ts|\.mts|\.cts$` regex won't match those resources, so TypeScript stripping would be skipped for such imports. Consider matching extensions before the `?` (e.g. use `module.nameForCondition()` or strip the query) and also use the same resource value for `sourceUrl` instead of `module.resource` so sourcemaps reflect `matchResource` when present.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

It should likely be `"application/typescript"` to match the default rule added in `lib/config/defaults.js` and to reflect a real mimetype value; otherwise the snapshot is asserting an impossible configuration.

body:
## Pull request overview This PR standardizes how deprecations are surfaced across the project by adding `@deprecated` JSDoc annotations in TypeScript typings, aligning JSON schema metadata for deprecated options, and adding/adjusting in-code deprecation markers and small internal refactors. **Changes:** - Added `@deprecated` annotations across various legacy APIs in `types.d.ts` and runtime JSDoc in `lib/Chunk.js` / `lib/Module.js`. - Updated option deprecation signaling in `declarations/WebpackOptions.d.ts` and `schemas/WebpackOptions.json` (including marking `exprContextCritical` as deprecated and removing an incorrect deprecation marker from `moduleIds`). - Small internal refactors: switch deprecated parser scoping API usages to `inFunctionScope`, and replace some `Buffer.slice` usages with `Buffer.subarray`. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 14 out of 16 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | types.d.ts | Adds `@deprecated` markers to legacy `Chunk`/`Module`/`Compilation` APIs and adjusts `assignDepths` typing. | | schemas/WebpackOptions.json | Marks `exprContextCritical` as deprecated and removes incorrect deprecation marker from `moduleIds`. | | lib/web/JsonpTemplatePlugin.js | Adds a webpack 6 removal TODO for a deprecated class. | | lib/serialization/FileMiddleware.js | Replaces `slice` with `subarray` in buffer handling paths. | | lib/dependencies/RequireEnsureDependenciesBlockParserPlugin.js | Replaces deprecated `parser.inScope` usage with `parser.inFunctionScope`. | | lib/dependencies/HarmonyImportDependency.js | Introduces a local typedef alias for `JavascriptParserOptions` and updates JSDoc accordingly. | | lib/dependencies/HarmonyExportDependencyParserPlugin.js | Introduces a local typedef alias for `JavascriptParserOptions` and updates JSDoc accordingly. | | lib/dependencies/AMDRequireDependenciesBlockParserPlugin.js | Replaces deprecated `parser.inScope` usage with `parser.inFunctionScope`. | | lib/dependencies/AMDDefineDependencyParserPlugin.js | Replaces deprecated `parser.inScope` usage with `parser.inFunctionScope`. | | lib/ModuleParseError.js | Uses `subarray` for WASM header detection on Buffer sources. | | lib/Module.js | Adds `@deprecated` JSDoc on legacy accessors and updates typedef imports. | | lib/Compilation.js | Switches included-module depth assignment to `assignDepths([module])` and updates JSDoc param type. | | lib/ChunkGraph.js | Adds explicit BACKWARD-COMPAT start/end markers around deprecated compat APIs. | | lib/Chunk.js | Adds `@deprecated` JSDoc to legacy methods (matching type deprecations). | | declarations/WebpackOptions.d.ts | Marks `exprContextCritical` as deprecated and removes incorrect `@deprecated` on `moduleIds`. | | .changeset/jolly-groups-tap.md | Adds a patch changeset describing the deprecation annotation updates. |

body: The PR description mentions covering “relative and absolute path-joining logic in the `afterResolve` phase,” but the added fixtures only explicitly exercise a relative string replacement (`"./after.js"`). If you intended to also cover the plugin’s handling of an absolute replacement string, add a dedicated case that sets the replacement to an absolute path (e.g., `path.resolve(__dirname, "after.js")`) and assert it resolves correctly.

## Pull request overview This PR fixes a watch-mode caching bug where runtime chunks could embed stale filename/hash references by ensuring initial chunks are hashed before runtime chunks during `Compilation.createHash()`. **Changes:** - Adjust chunk hashing order in `Compilation.createHash()` to hash initial chunks before runtime chunks. - Add a new watch regression test case covering runtime chunk filename map updates across rebuilds. - Add a changeset entry documenting the patch change. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 8 out of 8 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | lib/Compilation.js | Reorders chunk hashing so non-runtime (incl. initial) chunks are hashed before runtime chunks. | | test/watchCases/cache/stale-runtime-chunk-filename-map/webpack.config.js | Adds a dedicated watch-mode repro config with splitChunks + memory cache to exercise runtime filename mapping updates. | | test/watchCases/cache/stale-runtime-chunk-filename-map/test.config.js | Exposes a helper to read the emitted service worker asset for runtime-reference assertions. | | test/watchCases/cache/stale-runtime-chunk-filename-map/0/service-worker.js | Asserts that the runtime output references the current hashed shared chunk filename on each watch step. | | test/watchCases/cache/stale-runtime-chunk-filename-map/0/offscreen.js | Provides a second entry to force a shared initial chunk used by multiple entrypoints. | | test/watchCases/cache/stale-runtime-chunk-filename-map/0/shared.js | Baseline shared module for step 0. | | test/watchCases/cache/stale-runtime-chunk-filename-map/1/shared.js | Modified shared module for step 1 to force a rebuild and new chunk hash/filename. | | .changeset/public-horses-visit.md | Records the behavior change as a patch-level release note. |

body:
`optimization.realContentHash` is set to `false`, which disables `RealContentHashPlugin` (it is only applied when `realContentHash` is truthy). Since the reported regression manifests as a `RealContentHashPlugin` stale-reference error in watch mode, this test may not actually exercise the original failure mode. Consider leaving `realContentHash` enabled (default in production) so the regression test fails pre-fix and passes with this change, or update the test/PR description to reflect that it validates the underlying runtime filename map behavior without involving `RealContentHashPlugin`.
```suggestion

```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: This async loader helper doesn’t `return`/`await` the `import()` promises. Because `load1/load2` resolve immediately, failures won’t be caught by the `.catch(...)` handlers and the CSS chunks may not be loaded before the snapshot assertions run. Return the `import()` promise (or use top-level `await Promise.all([...])`) so the test reliably waits for both chunks to load.

body: ## Pull request overview This PR aims to improve developer-facing diagnostics for misconfigurations by adding entry validation in `EntryOptionPlugin` and making an internal “empty dependency” error more descriptive. **Changes:** - Expands the “Empty dependency (no request)” error message in `NormalModuleFactory`. - Adds validation in `EntryOptionPlugin` for non-object entries and for entry descriptions missing an `import` field. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | lib/NormalModuleFactory.js | Makes the empty-dependency error message more actionable. | | lib/EntryOptionPlugin.js | Adds runtime validation and more explicit errors for invalid entry shapes. | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The new validation only checks that `desc.import` is truthy, but does not validate its type. If a caller passes an unnormalized entry description like `{ import: "./src/index.js" }`, the subsequent `for...of` will iterate over the string’s characters, producing incorrect entry plugins instead of failing fast. Since this code now explicitly validates entry shapes, it should also enforce `Array.isArray(desc.import)` (and ideally that it contains non-empty strings) or normalize string to a single-element array before iterating.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: PR description/title focus on EntryOptionPlugin, but this hunk also changes an error message in NormalModuleFactory. If this change is intentional, consider updating the PR description/title to reflect it; otherwise it may be better split into a separate PR to keep scope aligned.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The new error message asserts that “HTML files are not directly supported as entry points” and recommends html-webpack-plugin, but webpack entries can be non-JS modules when a loader/module type is configured (including HTML via html-loader/asset modules). This makes the message potentially incorrect/misleading for users who passed a non-object entry value. Consider rephrasing to describe the actual expectation here (normalized entry object/function) without making assumptions about file types or recommending third-party plugins.
```suggestion
					"Invalid entry configuration. The 'entry' option must be either a function or an object mapping entry names to entry descriptions. Please refer to the webpack configuration documentation for the supported formats of the 'entry' option."
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Yes, this change in `NormalModuleFactory` is intentional.

Both updates focus on improving error message clarity and developer experience, so I included them in the same PR to keep related improvements together.

Please let me know if you'd prefer this to be split into a separate PR.

body:
Good catch — you're right that a string value for `desc.import` would currently be iterated character by character.

I’ve updated the implementation to normalize `desc.import` into an array before iteration, and added validation to ensure each entry is a non-empty string.

Thanks for pointing this out!

body: ## Pull request overview This PR refines webpack’s type surface around `NormalModule` by introducing generics for parser/generator types and updating related APIs/hooks to reference the parameterized `NormalModule`, with the goal of enabling future module-type-specific typing improvements. **Changes:** - Make `NormalModule` generic over parser/parserOptions and generator/generatorOptions, and thread that through many `NormalModule`-consuming type signatures. - Add JSDoc generics to `NormalModuleFactory.getParser/createParser/getGenerator/createGenerator` and adjust implementations with casts. - Specialize `CssModule`’s relationship to `NormalModule` with concrete parser/generator types (JS JSDoc + `types.d.ts`). ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 3 out of 4 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `types.d.ts` | Introduces generic `NormalModule` and updates numerous signatures/hooks to use the parameterized form. | | `lib/NormalModuleFactory.js` | Adds JSDoc templates for parser/generator factory methods and returns typed casts. | | `lib/NormalModule.js` | Adds generics to `NormalModule` and its create-data typedef; updates internal casts accordingly. | | `lib/CssModule.js` | Adds typed `@extends` and create-data typing to bind `CssModule` to specific parser/generator types. | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
In the `NormalModuleCreateData` typedef, `parserOptions`/`generatorOptions` are modeled as `{PO | undefined}` and `{GO | undefined}` instead of being marked optional (e.g. `{PO=}` / `{GO=}` like other typedef object properties in this repo). This can force object literals to explicitly include these properties (often as `undefined`) under `checkJs`/`strict` and makes the typedef inconsistent with established optional-property JSDoc style here.
```suggestion
 * @property {PO=} parserOptions the options of the parser used
 * @property {G} generator the generator used
 * @property {GO=} generatorOptions the options of the generator used
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: In `_restoreFromUnsafeCache`, the casts force `unsafeCacheData.parserOptions`/`generatorOptions` to `PO`/`GO`, but these values can be `undefined` (and the instance fields are typed as `PO | undefined` / `GO | undefined`). Casting to `PO | undefined` / `GO | undefined` keeps the JSDoc types accurate under `checkJs`/`strict` and avoids masking incorrect assumptions about these fields always being present.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The `@template`-based return typing for `getParser`/`createParser` and `getGenerator`/`createGenerator` lets callers pick an arbitrary subtype for `P`/`G`, but the runtime selection is based only on the `type` string. This is effectively a typed cast and can hide real type mismatches (e.g., requesting `CssParser` for a JS module type). Consider returning the base `Parser`/`Generator` types here (or deriving the generic from `type` via a mapping) rather than letting callers specify it freely.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

It's expected, because we now add a `PureExpressionDependency` for unused top-level symbols which is one object or array, and these dependencies include runtime conditions. For multiple entries/runtimes, we rely on `RuntimeGlobals.runtimeId`(the newly added) to determine whether the current chunk's runtime actually uses the symbol.

body:
The problem statement is factually off: webpack entries are module requests and can point at non-JS resources via loaders; the missing piece here is *first-class* HTML entry semantics (parsing, dependency discovery, HTML output). Please reword to avoid claiming webpack only accepts JavaScript entry files.
```suggestion
Currently, while webpack entry points are generic module requests and can target non-JavaScript resources via loaders, webpack lacks native, first-class support for treating HTML files themselves as entry points (including HTML parsing, dependency discovery, and HTML output). This project adds such native support for HTML entry points.
```
