body: `HtmlGenerator` behavior changes when `extract` is left undefined (it auto-enables extraction for entry HTML modules via `_shouldExtract(module)`), but `updateHash` only hashes `this.options.extract`. This can make module hashes/caching insensitive to the effective extract decision and can lead to stale/missing HTML-type codegen results when a module becomes (or stops being) an entry. Consider hashing the *effective* extraction state (e.g. `this._shouldExtract(updateHashContext.module)`) instead of only the raw option value.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The renderManifest items use `identifier: htmlModule<moduleId>` and `hash: fullContentHash` computed from `placeholderContent` (before substituting the undoPath). If the same HTML module is present in multiple chunks and `output.htmlFilename` vs `output.htmlChunkFilename` (or directory depth) produce different `undoPath`/final HTML, two manifest entries can end up sharing the same (identifier, hash) cache key but needing different rendered content/filenames. This can cause asset-cache reuse with the wrong URL prefixes. Consider making the cache key reflect the emitted variant (e.g. include `filename` or `chunk.canBeInitial()`/template name in `identifier`, and/or compute `hash` from `finalContent`).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The comment says extraction runs for HTML modules whose generator has `extract: true`, but the implemented default also extracts when `extract` is unset and the HTML module is a compilation entry (`HtmlGenerator#_shouldExtract`). Please update this comment to avoid misleading readers about when extracted `.html` assets are emitted.

body: `output.htmlFilename` / `output.htmlChunkFilename` defaults are derived by swapping `.js` for `.html`. When `output.filename` is a constant like `bundle.js`, this yields a constant `bundle.html`, which can conflict if multiple HTML modules are extracted in the same compilation (e.g. `module.generator.html.extract: true` and importing more than one `.html` file). Consider mirroring the `chunkFilename` uniqueness logic: if the derived template doesn't contain a per-module differentiator like `[name]`, `[id]`, or a hash placeholder, fall back to a unique template (e.g. `[name].html` or `[id].html`) to avoid asset name collisions by default.

body: `isStylesheetEntry` is computed per-element, but this loop runs per-attribute (e.g. `<link>` can also have `imagesrcset`). With the current `isEntry = isStylesheetEntry || …` logic, a `<link rel="stylesheet" imagesrcset="…">` would incorrectly treat the `imagesrcset` URLs as CSS entry chunks. Scope the stylesheet-entry promotion to the `href` attribute only (e.g. include `attributeName === "href"` in the condition) so other `<link>` URL attributes remain normal asset rewrites.

body: `generateError` for `html` currently injects `error.message` into an HTML comment without escaping. If the message contains `-->` or other HTML, the output can break out of the comment and produce unintended markup/script execution when the extracted HTML is served. Please escape/sanitize the message for HTML (or wrap a safely escaped JSON string) before embedding it.

body: The `finalContentHash` calculation mixes `hashSalt` into the input via string concatenation (`${salt}|${content}`), which differs from the hashing scheme used elsewhere in webpack (typically `hash.update(salt); hash.update(content)`). For consistency and to avoid edge cases with salts that contain separators, update the hash with `hashSalt` in a separate `update()` call before hashing `finalContent`.

## Pull request overview This PR updates module request handling so `#` characters in directory names are escaped before normal resource resolution, addressing failures when paths with `#` also include query strings. **Changes:** - Adds `escapeHashInPathRequest` in `lib/util/identifier.js`. - Applies the escaping in `NormalModuleFactory` before resolving resources. - Adds unit/config tests and a patch changeset for issue #16819. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 8 out of 8 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `lib/util/identifier.js` | Adds hash escaping helper for path-like requests with query strings. | | `lib/NormalModuleFactory.js` | Uses the new helper before resolving unresolved resources. | | `test/identifier.unittest.js` | Adds unit coverage for hash escaping behavior. | | `test/configCases/parsing/issue-16819-#-in-path-#/webpack.config.js` | Adds absolute-path regression config. | | `test/configCases/parsing/issue-16819-#-in-path-#/index.js` | Adds regression test entry. | | `test/configCases/parsing/issue-16819-relative-#/webpack.config.js` | Adds relative-path regression config. | | `test/configCases/parsing/issue-16819-relative-#/#dir/index.js` | Adds relative regression test entry. | | `.changeset/parse-resource-hash-in-absolute-path.md` | Adds patch release note. |
--- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: This treats any `#` followed by another path separator before a later `?` as a directory hash, but webpack's existing parser allows fragments such as `#/some/fragment?query` (the current tests already include `./resourceFragment/index#/some/fragment` as a fragment-like request). Such requests would now be rewritten to `\0#`, changing the path/query split and breaking existing fragment semantics. The escape should only apply when the `?` is known to be a query separator for the path, not when it is part of a `#...` fragment.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: This release note says `parseResource` now handles absolute paths containing `#`, but the exported parser is unchanged and still splits an unescaped request like `/home/user/proj#1/file.js?q=1` at the `#`. The fix only escapes requests before normal resolution, so the changelog should avoid promising direct `parseResource` behavior that is not implemented.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The case you describe (`#/some/fragment?query` parsed as a fragment containing both `/` and `?`) is structurally identical to the actual bug case in #16819: `/home/user/proj#/webpack/.../index.js?protocol=ws...`. Both are "a segment ending with `#`, followed by `/...?query`". Pure string analysis cannot distinguish "directory `#` + query" from "fragment containing `/` + `?` inside the fragment" — both interpretations are valid URL syntax, webpack has historically supported both, and the parser has no filesystem context.

Given the ambiguity, we have to choose. This fix biases toward the directory interpretation because:
- It is the case from #16819, which has been open since 2023 and affects anyone whose project path contains `#` plus uses webpack-dev-server.
- The fragment-with-`/`-and-`?` pattern is webpack-specific and rare. Existing tests using fragment semantics (e.g. `./resourceFragment/index#" + __dirname`) don't include a query string, so none of them break.
- The escape is gated on `?` being present; requests without a query keep their existing semantics entirely, so the common fragment cases are untouched.
- Users who genuinely want fragment-containing-`/`-and-`?` semantics can still write `\0#` explicitly to opt out.

The changeset is being updated separately to remove the `parseResource` mention (your other comment) — that wording was stale.

## Pull request overview This PR adds Node-compatible CommonJS `require(esm)` interop for ES modules that export a binding named `"module.exports"`, returning that export instead of the namespace object. **Changes:** - Adds shared helper logic for detecting and generating `"module.exports"` unwrapping. - Wires the unwrap into CommonJS require, property/call require chains, and CJS re-export dependency templates. - Adds a config case covering plain require, property access, calls, destructuring, re-exports, wrappers, and native Node comparison. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 19 out of 19 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `.changeset/require-esm-module-exports-named-export.md` | Documents the new interop behavior. | | `lib/dependencies/CommonJsDependencyHelpers.js` | Adds the shared `"module.exports"` detection/access helper. | | `lib/dependencies/CommonJsRequireDependency.js` | Applies unwrap access to plain `require()` and referenced exports. | | `lib/dependencies/CommonJsImportsParserPlugin.js` | Passes the full require-call range for unwrap insertion. | | `lib/dependencies/CommonJsFullRequireDependency.js` | Applies unwrap to chained property/call require expressions. | | `lib/dependencies/CommonJsExportRequireDependency.js` | Applies unwrap to CJS re-export patterns. | | `test/configCases/require/esm-module-exports/*` | Adds fixtures and tests for Node-compatible behavior across require shapes and wrappers. |
--- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `getRequireEsmModuleExportsAccess` is also called from `getReferencedExports()`, before the dependency has marked `"module.exports"` as used. In builds with usage information enabled, `getUsedName(["module.exports"], runtime)` can return `false` at that point, so property/destructuring requires fall back to referencing the accessed property (for example `named`) instead of `"module.exports"`; the unwrap can then be skipped or the special export can be tree-shaken. Split the eligibility check from the used-name lookup (or allow this helper to ignore usage when computing referenced exports) so `getReferencedExports()` always reports `[["module.exports"]]` whenever the export is provided.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Good catch — reproduced and fixed in 13aa68a.

Confirmed via a standalone webpack build with `mode: "production", usedExports: true` and a fixture where `"module.exports"` and `named` are bound to different values: webpack emitted `__webpack_require__(id).named` and returned `"named-value"`, while Node's `require(esm)` unwraps first and then accesses `.named` on the string, yielding `undefined`.

Split the helper into two:

- `isRequireEsmModuleExportsModule(module, moduleGraph)` — eligibility only (module type + provided), no usage lookup. Used by all three `getReferencedExports` paths and `getExports`.
- `getRequireEsmModuleExportsAccess(module, moduleGraph, runtime)` — calls the eligibility check, then `getUsedName`. Used only at template-apply time, when usage info is final.

The config now enables `optimization.usedExports: true` and a new `distinct.mjs` fixture pins this down on every run.

## Pull request overview This PR fixes a parsing edge case where, in Harmony/ESM modules, `HarmonyDetectionParserPlugin` short-circuited `define(...)` calls and inadvertently skipped walking their arguments. That prevented imported bindings referenced inside inline AMD factory callbacks (or via callback identifiers passed to `define`) from being tracked, which could lead to incorrect tree-shaking and even runtime `ReferenceError`s. **Changes:** - Update `HarmonyDetectionParserPlugin` to walk `define(...)`/`exports(...)` call arguments in Harmony modules before skipping default AMD/CommonJS handling. - Add a parsing test case to ensure default/named imports used inside `define` callbacks are correctly linked. - Add an inner-graph configCase to ensure tree-shaking keeps the correct imported bindings when `define` callbacks reference them. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 6 out of 6 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `lib/dependencies/HarmonyDetectionParserPlugin.js` | Walks `define`/`exports` call arguments in Harmony modules to ensure import binding references inside callbacks are discovered. | | `test/cases/parsing/issue-17063/index.js` | Regression test validating import bindings are linked when referenced inside `define(...)` callbacks. | | `test/cases/parsing/issue-17063/lib.js` | Provides default and named exports used by the parsing regression test. | | `test/configCases/inner-graph/issue-17063/module.js` | Inner-graph regression reproducer for tree-shaking of imports used inside `define` callbacks / passed callbacks. | | `test/configCases/inner-graph/issue-17063/webpack.config.js` | Adds the inner-graph regression assertions for used exports. | | `.changeset/fix-import-binding-in-define.md` | Patch changeset describing the fix and affected patterns. |

## Pull request overview This PR addresses a stack overflow in side-effects analysis on very deep chains of side-effect-free imports by replacing recursive descent in `NormalModule.getSideEffectsConnectionState` with an iterative trampoline driving a generator-based walk (fixing issue #20986) while aiming to preserve existing tree-shaking semantics. **Changes:** - Refactor side-effects connection state evaluation to an iterative trampoline + generator walk to avoid V8 call stack overflows. - Add unit tests covering a 20,000-deep chain, cycle handling, and bailout propagation. - Add an integration config case that generates a ~500-module import chain and verifies compilation doesn’t overflow the stack; include a changeset entry. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 6 out of 6 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.

body: The `import()` inside this test will actually initiate chunk loading at runtime. In the configCases harness, scripts aren’t auto-evaluated by default, so this can leave a pending chunk-load timeout and eventually trigger an unhandled rejection later in the test run. Since the magic comment only needs to exist in the module to affect compilation, wrap the `import()` in unreachable code (e.g. `if (Math.random() < -1)`) or otherwise avoid executing it at runtime.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: This `import()` executes at runtime and can start an unresolved chunk load (including a long chunkLoadTimeout) in the test harness, which may later surface as an unhandled rejection. The test only needs the import statement for compilation to create the shared named chunk group; consider placing it in unreachable code (or otherwise preventing execution) to avoid side effects during the test run.

## Pull request overview This PR updates webpack’s experimental HTML tokenizer (`walkHtmlTokens`) to more closely follow the WHATWG HTML tokenization spec and match behavior of `swc_html_parser`, including fixing multiple byte-range edge cases and expanding entity decoding to the full named character reference table. **Changes:** - Align tokenizer state-machine behavior for script-data escaping, content-mode close tags via attribute paths, markup-declaration EOF handling, and missing attribute values; add a new `parseError` callback with `"warning"`/`"error"` severities. - Implement spec-correct named character reference matching (full WHATWG entities table + longest-prefix backtrack) and upgrade `decodeHtmlEntities` accordingly. - Add a generator (`tooling/generate-html-entities.js`) plus extensive unit tests (targeting full branch coverage). ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 6 out of 7 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `lib/html/walkHtmlTokens.js` | Main tokenizer updates: state-machine fixes, new `parseError` callback, full WHATWG entity table + improved entity decoding, and expanded EOF handling. | | `tooling/generate-html-entities.js` | Generates the inlined `HTML_ENTITIES` region in `walkHtmlTokens.js` from `tooling/html-entities.json`. | | `package.json` | Hooks the generator into `lint:special` / `fix:special` workflows. | | `tooling/html-entities.json` | Vendored WHATWG named character references table used by the generator. | | `test/walkHtmlTokens.unittest.js` | Adds regression tests and broad state-machine branch coverage + parseError/decodeHtmlEntities tests. | | `cspell.json` | Excludes `tooling/html-entities.json` from spellchecking. | | `.changeset/align-html-lexer-script-data.md` | Patch changeset documenting tokenizer alignment, new callback, and full entities support. |

body: STATE_SCRIPT_DATA_DOUBLE_ESCAPE_END now appends to `tempBuffer` unconditionally. In worst-case inputs with a very long ASCII-alpha run after `</` this grows without bound and repeatedly concatenates strings, which can cause large memory use and quadratic-time behavior. You can preserve the spec behavior for matching exactly "script" while keeping this bounded by capping the buffer at ~7 chars (or tracking a `tooLong` flag) so longer names still never equal "script".
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The named-character-reference scan cap looks inconsistent: the loop increments `runLen` and then breaks on `runLen > 32`, which allows consuming 33 alphanumeric chars. Either the cap should be adjusted (e.g. break at `>= 32` / `> 33`) or the comment should be updated so the bound is unambiguous and matches the intended maximum entity-name length.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `decodeHtmlEntities` does longest-prefix matching by iterating from `name.length` down to 1 and allocating `slice(0, i)` each step. For inputs like `&` + a very long alphanumeric run, this becomes expensive (many large substring allocations). Since WHATWG entity names have a known maximum length, consider bounding the search to that max (and still append the untouched remainder) to keep decoding linear-time for pathological inputs.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `decodeHtmlEntities`' numeric-reference regex currently treats decimal numeric references as if they could contain hex digits (it matches `[0-9a-fA-F]+` even when there's no `x`). This causes inputs like `"&#65b"` (or `"&#1f"`) to be decoded incorrectly and to drop the trailing character(s) that should remain as literal text. Update the matcher/parsing so decimal references only consume `[0-9]+` and hex references only consume `[0-9a-fA-F]+` when prefixed by `x`/`X`, and add a regression test (e.g. `"&#65b"` -> `"Ab"`).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The test name/comment says named character reference consumption is capped at 33 characters, but the implementation uses `MAX_ENTITY_NAME_LEN = 32` (and caps the alphanumeric run at `MAX_ENTITY_NAME_LEN - 1`). Please update the test description/comment to match the actual cap to avoid confusion when maintaining this logic.

`REGION_REGEXP` hard-codes `

body: Redirect handling uses `res.headers.location` as-is; HTTP redirects are allowed to return relative `Location` values, which would make `https.get` throw on an invalid URL. Consider resolving with `new URL(location, url).toString()` (and validating `location` is present) before recursing.

body: The new `parseError` API and related comments/JSDoc describe `start`/`end` as “byte offsets” / “byte ranges”, but these indices are JS string offsets (UTF-16 code units) since all calculations use `input.length`, `slice`, and `charCodeAt`. To avoid misleading consumers (e.g. for non-BMP characters), consider renaming the docs to “string offsets” (or explicitly “UTF-16 code unit offsets”) throughout.

body: Entity table lookups should not rely on `HTML_ENTITIES[key] !== undefined` with a normal object, because prototype properties like `toString`/`constructor`/`__proto__` will be treated as “present” even though they are not real entities. Use a null-prototype table (e.g. `Object.assign(Object.create(null), …)` in the generated block) or `Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(HTML_ENTITIES, key)` for the match check so unknown entities like `&toString;` don’t falsely match.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Same issue as above: `HTML_ENTITIES[bare] !== undefined` can succeed for keys inherited from `Object.prototype` (e.g. `toString`). Ensure the entity table has a null prototype or use an own-property check before treating a prefix as a valid named character reference.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `decodeHtmlEntities` also uses `HTML_ENTITIES[prefix] !== undefined` for matches. With a normal object this will incorrectly “match” inherited properties like `toString`/`constructor`, producing incorrect decoding output. Use an own-property check or a null-prototype `HTML_ENTITIES` table (preferably generated) to make lookups safe.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The generated `HTML_ENTITIES` literal uses `Object.freeze(${JSON.stringify(map)})`, which creates an object with `Object.prototype`. That makes lookups using `obj[key] !== undefined` unsafe because keys like `toString`/`constructor`/`__proto__` appear “present” via the prototype chain. Consider emitting a null-prototype table (e.g. `Object.freeze(Object.assign(Object.create(null), …))`) so consumers can do fast bracket lookups safely.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Grammar: the message should read “needs to be updated” (singular file path) rather than “need to be updated”.

body: This changeset description refers to “byte-range” and “byte range”, but `walkHtmlTokens` (and the new `parseError` callback) uses JS string indices (UTF-16 code-unit offsets), not byte offsets. Consider rewording to “offset range”/“string index range” to avoid misleading consumers, especially for non-ASCII input.

In Node's http(s) client this can leave the socket unread and cause resource leaks/hangs on subsequent requests. Consider calling `res.resume()` (or `res.destroy()`) before following redirects / rejecting on non-200 so the response stream is properly drained/closed.

body: In the EOF mid-tag handler, `tagNameEnd` is only forced to `len` when `state === STATE_TAG_NAME`. If EOF occurs while in a content-mode end-tag name state (e.g. `STATE_RCDATA_END_TAG_NAME` / `STATE_RAWTEXT_END_TAG_NAME` / `STATE_SCRIPT_DATA*_END_TAG_NAME`), `tagNameEnd` may still be `-1` or a stale value from a previous tag, causing `emitCloseTag(len)` to slice the wrong tag name at EOF. Set `tagNameEnd = len` for these end-tag-name states as well (or more generally when `tagNameStart !== -1` and `tagNameEnd < tagNameStart`).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: In the EOF handler for mid-tag states, calling `flushText(tagStart)` can re-emit already-flushed text. For example, when parsing `a<div` at EOF, the transition into `STATE_TAG_NAME` already called `flushText(tagStart)` in `STATE_TAG_OPEN`, but this EOF path calls it again, causing duplicate `text` callbacks for the same range. Consider removing this `flushText(tagStart)` here (or otherwise ensuring the text span is only flushed once, e.g. by advancing `textStart` when flushing on tag/comment entry).

## Pull request overview This PR fixes an interaction bug in `SourceMapDevToolPlugin` that prevented multiple sourcemap producers (implicit `devtool`, multiple plugin instances, or both) from operating on the same emitted asset, which could previously lead to skipped processing and/or empty `.map` output. **Changes:** - Add a compilation-scoped registry (via a module-level `WeakMap`) to allow later plugin instances to recover the original source map even after an earlier instance rewraps the asset. - Namespace persistent cache entries with an options-derived salt to avoid cross-instance cache collisions on the same asset. - Preserve/append `assetInfo.related.sourceMap` instead of overwriting it, enabling multiple sourcemap references per asset. - Add a config case to validate `devtool + plugin` and `plugin + plugin` multi-map generation. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 6 out of 6 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `lib/SourceMapDevToolPlugin.js` | Enables multi-instance/devtool coexistence via original-source registry, cache-key salting, non-mutating map output, and `related.sourceMap` aggregation. | | `test/configCases/source-map/devtool-and-source-map-plugin/webpack.config.js` | Adds a multi-compiler config covering `devtool + plugin` and dual plugin instances emitting different map variants. | | `test/configCases/source-map/devtool-and-source-map-plugin/test.config.js` | Ensures both output bundles are executed by the config-case harness. | | `test/configCases/source-map/devtool-and-source-map-plugin/primary.js` | Asserts the `devtool` map and a secondary `noSources` map are both emitted and only the explicit plugin appends the URL comment. | | `test/configCases/source-map/devtool-and-source-map-plugin/dual.js` | Asserts two plugin instances can emit two maps for the same asset and that mappings match between them. | | `.changeset/multi-source-map-devtool-plugin.md` | Adds a patch changeset documenting the behavior change and the resolved scenario from #6813. |

body: `extractSourceAndMap` is documented as returning `{ source: string | Buffer, ... }`, but it immediately bails out unless `source` is a string. This makes the JSDoc inaccurate and can also pin `asset` into the registry even when the function ultimately returns `undefined` due to a non-string source. Consider updating the return type to `source: string` and/or moving the `typeof source !== "string"` check before registering/pinning to keep behavior and docs consistent.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `serializeRule` is intended to serialize `Rules` values, but the JSDoc uses `EXPECTED_ANY` even though a `Rules` typedef is available in this file. Tightening the JSDoc type to `Rules` (or a union matching the supported rule shapes) would improve readability and catch misuse in tooling/type-checking.

body: The `_cacheSalt` string is built by joining serialized option values with a literal `|`. Since option values (e.g. `publicPath`, `fileContext`, templates) can themselves contain `|`, different option sets can collide into the same salt string (e.g. segments `["s:a", "s:b|s:c"]` vs `["s:a|s:b", "s:c"]`). That can cause incorrect persistent-cache hits and wrong source map outputs. Consider generating the salt via an unambiguous encoding (e.g. `JSON.stringify([...])`, or length-prefixed/escaped segments) so cache keys remain collision-resistant.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:

author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The persistent cache salt doesn’t include `options.namespace`, but `namespace` affects the generated source names (`webpack://[namespace]/...`) and therefore the emitted source map contents. With two SourceMapDevToolPlugin instances targeting the same asset but different `namespace` values, they’ll still share the same cache identifier and keep invalidating/overwriting each other’s entries. Include `options.namespace` in `_cacheSalt` (or otherwise incorporate the resolved namespace into the cache identifier) so these variants don’t collide.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The cache identifier is built as ```${file}|${this._cacheSalt}```, but the PR rationale explicitly avoids homemade separators due to collision risk. Asset filenames can legally contain `|` on some platforms/configurations, which could make two different (file,salt) pairs map to the same identifier string. Consider switching to a collision-safe encoding (e.g., `JSON.stringify([file, saltParts])` or a delimiter that cannot appear in filenames) for the identifier construction.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

When `options.debugIds` is enabled, this code prepends `debugIdComment` to `currentSourceMappingURLComment` via template-string concatenation. If `options.append` was provided as a function (supported by the schema), this will coerce the function to a string and break the expected behavior of calling the function to generate the final comment.

Consider handling the function case explicitly: if `currentSourceMappingURLComment` is a function, wrap it in a new function that returns `debugIdComment + originalFn(pathData)`; if it's a string, prepend as today.

That will cause each compilation run to try to execute bundles that weren't emitted for that config index (and duplicate tests). Adjust `findBundle` to use `i`/`options` so each config only returns its own output filename (e.g. based on `options.output.filename` or a switch on `i`).

body: ## Pull request overview Fixes the experimental HTML-entry pipeline (`experiments.html` + `experiments.css`) so that CSS assets reachable from an HTML `
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `chunkHasCss` claims to mirror `CssModulesPlugin.chunkHasCss`, but it only checks `CSS_TYPE` and ignores `CSS_IMPORT_TYPE`. Chunks containing only `css-import` modules would be missed, so required stylesheet assets could be omitted from the extracted HTML and ordering could be wrong. Consider importing `CSS_IMPORT_TYPE` and treating either source type as CSS here (and in `firstCssModulePostOrderIndex` if you want ordering to reflect `@import` modules too).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `chunkHasJs` only checks for `JAVASCRIPT_TYPE` modules. Runtime chunks created via `optimization.runtimeChunk` often contain only `runtime` modules (source type "runtime"), so this can return false even though a `.js` file must be loaded before other chunks. This can break tag ordering when both `runtimeChunk` and `splitChunks` are enabled (vendor chunk emitted before runtime). Consider including `chunk.hasRuntime()` and/or checking for `WEBPACK_MODULE_TYPE_RUNTIME`/entry modules similar to `JavascriptModulesPlugin`’s logic when deciding whether a chunk needs a `<script>` tag.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The `jsChunks` vs `otherChunks` split can reorder JS execution: chunks that are neither detected as CSS nor JS (including runtime-only chunks when `chunkHasJs` returns false) are emitted after `jsChunks`, even though `getEntrypointChunksInLoadOrder` puts runtime first. This can produce HTML that loads a dependent JS chunk before its runtime (`__webpack_require__ is not defined`). Consider ensuring all JS-emitting chunks (incl. runtime) stay in the original `orderedChunks` relative order, even while placing CSS `<link>` tags before all `<script>` tags.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `cssChunkOrder.sort((a, b) => a.index - b.index)` can produce ties (e.g. when `firstCssModulePostOrderIndex` returns `Infinity` for multiple chunks). On legacy Node/V8 (still supported by this repo), `Array.prototype.sort` tie ordering isn’t guaranteed to be stable, which could make emitted `<link>` order non-deterministic across environments. Consider adding a deterministic tie-breaker (e.g. by `chunk.id`/`chunk.name`).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: New ordering logic is covered for CSS-only split chunks and mixed link+script cases, but there isn’t a config case that combines `optimization.runtimeChunk` + `optimization.splitChunks` + JS-imported CSS. Given the new JS/CSS grouping logic, adding a regression test for that combination would help catch cases where the runtime `<script>` (and any runtime chunk CSS) is emitted in the wrong order relative to vendor/entry chunks.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `firstCssModulePostOrderIndex` only considers `CSS_TYPE` modules when computing the ordering key. Chunks that emit CSS exclusively via `CSS_IMPORT_TYPE` (e.g. pure `@import` placeholder modules) will then get an index of `Infinity` and be sorted by the tie-breaker instead of true source order, which can break cascade ordering. Consider incorporating both `CSS_TYPE` and `CSS_IMPORT_TYPE` when scanning modules for the minimum post-order index.

body: `cssChunkOrder.sort` uses `const d = a.index - b.index;` as the primary comparator. When both indices are `Number.POSITIVE_INFINITY` (the documented fallback for chunks with no reachable CSS module), `Infinity - Infinity` yields `NaN`, causing the comparator to return `NaN` and skip the tie-breaker. This can reintroduce unstable / non-deterministic ordering (especially on Node 10 where `Array#sort` stability isn’t guaranteed). Use explicit comparisons (`<`/`>`) or guard against `NaN` so the tie-breaker always runs when indices are equal (including `Infinity`).

## Pull request overview This PR improves CommonJS tree-shaking when a module is imported via a named `const` binding (`const ns = require("./mod")`). It does so by tracking that binding in the parser and forwarding subsequent *static* member accesses on the binding to the same `CommonJsRequireDependency`, enabling `usedExports` to drop unused `exports.*` assignments while still bailing out to full-namespace usage when the binding is used in non-static ways. **Changes:** - Track `const NAME = require("literal")` bindings and record later static member-chain reads/calls (`NAME.x`, `NAME["x"]`, `NAME.x()`) as referenced exports on the underlying `CommonJsRequireDependency`. - Add regression tests for issue #14054 and a dedicated `cjs-tree-shaking/require-member-access` case to validate partial namespace usage and bailout behavior. - Add a changeset documenting the patch-level behavior change. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 10 out of 10 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `lib/dependencies/CommonJsImportsParserPlugin.js` | Tags `const` require bindings and forwards static member-chain usage into `CommonJsRequireDependency.referencedExports`, with bailout to full namespace on value-usage. | | `test/configCases/issues/issue-14054/webpack.config.js` | Production config for the regression case validating minified output markers. | | `test/configCases/issues/issue-14054/index.js` | Regression assertions that unused CommonJS exports are removed when only static members are accessed. | | `test/configCases/issues/issue-14054/utils.js` | Fixture module with used/unused exported markers. | | `test/configCases/issues/issue-14054/utils-fullref.js` | Fixture module for the “full namespace read” counter-test (ensures exports are retained). | | `test/cases/cjs-tree-shaking/require-member-access/test.filter.js` | Skips this case in development mode where `usedExports` analysis is not active. | | `test/cases/cjs-tree-shaking/require-member-access/index.js` | Verifies used-exports tracking through a require binding and a bailout scenario via value usage. | | `test/cases/cjs-tree-shaking/require-member-access/module.js` | Exposes `__webpack_exports_info__.usedExports` for assertions. | | `test/cases/cjs-tree-shaking/require-member-access/module-rest.js` | Same fixture used for the bailout (namespace read) test. | | `.changeset/cjs-require-binding-tree-shake.md` | Patch changeset describing the new tree-shaking behavior for `const NAME = require(...)` bindings. |

## Pull request overview

Copilot reviewed 72 out of 75 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The new `let` capability check uses different minimum versions than the existing `const` check (e.g. `const` allows `firefox: 36` / `android: 37`, but `let` requires `firefox: 44` / `android: 49`). This can yield `tp.const === true` while `tp.let === false`, which contradicts the assumption in `applyOutputDefaults` that `let` should fall back to `const` unless the target *doesn't report* it. Unless there’s a documented engine bug requiring stricter thresholds for `let`, consider aligning the `let` version map with the `const` one (or deriving `let` directly from `const`) to avoid incorrectly disabling `output.environment.let` for targets that already support it.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The stricter `let` thresholds are intentional and reflect real engine support, so I've kept them and instead corrected the misleading comment in `applyOutputDefaults` that implied `const` always implies `let`.

Standard block-scoped `let` shipped later than `const` in some engines — e.g. Firefox got reliable block-scoped `let` in 44 (and Android WebView in 49), whereas webpack has tracked `const` from Firefox 36 / Android 37 (those Firefox numbers and notes predate this PR). So for a Firefox 36–43 browserslist target, `const: true` / `let: false` is the *correct* result, and we genuinely want to emit `const` but not `let` there.

The `conditionallyOptimistic(tp.let, environment.const)` fallback is purely for backward compatibility: it only kicks in when a target doesn't report `let` *at all* (`undefined`) — i.e. older/custom target objects that predate this capability and only declare `const`. Targets that do report `let` (browserslist and the built-in presets in `target.js`) keep their own accurate value, so there's no contradiction. Aligning the `let` map to the `const` versions would incorrectly claim `let` support for Firefox 36–43.

body: ## Pull request overview This PR adds Hot Module Replacement behavior for experimental HTML modules by emitting an HMR-aware JS shim (self-accept + optional DOM patching when the HTML is extracted), and expands the hot test suite to cover common HTML dependency patterns. **Changes:** - Emit an HMR self-accepting JS shim for `.html` modules, with DOM patching (`document.body.innerHTML` / `document.title`) when the HTML is extracted. - Prevent HTML module concatenation when HMR is enabled to preserve per-module `module.hot` scope. - Add hot test cases for import-only HTML, extracted HTML DOM patching + reload fallback, and external/inline `
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: In the generated HMR shim, `__webpack_strip_comments__` is used to build `__webpack_stripped__`, and the body/title are extracted from that stripped string. This means the DOM-patched `document.body.innerHTML` will drop any real HTML comments present in the `<body>` (not just decoy tags), so the patched DOM no longer matches the updated HTML. Consider only stripping/masking comments for tag *detection* (to avoid matching `<body>`/`</body>` inside comments) while extracting the actual body/title substring from the original HTML so comments are preserved in the patched output.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `data.__webpack_html_updated__ = true` is written in the dispose handler, but nothing in the shim reads it (only `data.__webpack_head__` is used). This adds extra output and implies state that doesn't exist. Either remove this flag or use it for an explicit guard, but avoid leaving unused data fields in the emitted runtime.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `innerHTML` should coerce the assigned value the same way the real DOM does (e.g. `el.innerHTML = 0` becomes "0"). Using `String(value || "")` converts falsy non-null values like `0`/`false` to an empty string. Prefer a nullish check (`value == null ? "" : String(value)`) to better match browser behavior and avoid surprising test harness behavior.

body:
For extracted HTML modules, the DOM-patch HMR shim uses the JS-export HTML string produced by `_renderHtml(..., "")`, which strips `[webpack/auto]` placeholders to an empty string. That differs from the extracted `.html` asset, where `HtmlModulesPlugin` resolves `[webpack/auto]` to an undoPath based on the emitted HTML filename. If `output.htmlFilename` emits into a subdirectory (e.g. `pages/[name].html`), a hot update that patches `document.body.innerHTML` can rewrite URLs (e.g. `<script src>`, `<link href>`, asset URLs) from `../...` to `...`, breaking resource resolution after HMR.

Consider keeping `[webpack/auto]` in the HMR/DOM-patch variant and resolving it at runtime (e.g. to `__webpack_require__.p`/publicPath), or otherwise ensuring the patched HTML uses the same undoPath semantics as the extracted HTML asset.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Good catch — fixed in a214a07. The extract HMR shim now keeps the `[webpack/auto]` placeholder in the body HTML and resolves it at runtime against `__webpack_require__.p` (the shim builds `__webpack_html__` via `.split("__WEBPACK_CSS_PUBLIC_PATH_AUTO__").join(__webpack_require__.p)`, and `generate` adds `RuntimeGlobals.publicPath` to the runtime requirements). So a DOM-patched `document.body` now points at the output root regardless of where the extracted `.html` document lives, matching the undo-path semantics `HtmlModulesPlugin` applies to the extracted asset. Added `test/hotCases/html/html-extract-public-path/` covering `publicPath:"auto"` + a body `<script src>` to lock in that the placeholder is resolved at runtime and never leaks into the patched DOM.

body:
Update: after rebasing this branch onto the latest `main`, the runtime-resolution fix I described above was dropped. `main` reworked HTML placeholder handling — chunk URLs are emitted as sentinels and a `JavascriptModulesPlugin.render` tap resolves them and blanket-replaces `[webpack/auto]` to root-relative URLs across the entire rendered JS chunk at build time. That tap is incompatible with a runtime `.split("[webpack/auto]").join(__webpack_require__.p)` in the shim (it rewrites the literal placeholder argument itself), and it makes the JS export deliberately root-relative. The HMR DOM-patch now reuses that same build-time-resolved HTML, so it's consistent with the non-HMR JS export rather than fighting `main`'s mechanism. Added `test/hotCases/html/html-extract-body-asset/` to cover a body `<script src>` being DOM-patched as a resolved chunk URL.

## Pull request overview

Copilot reviewed 69 out of 69 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
For the extracted+DOM-patch HMR path, `generate()` builds `generated` via `_renderHtml(..., "")`, which strips `CssUrlDependency.PUBLIC_PATH_AUTO` (`__WEBPACK_CSS_PUBLIC_PATH_AUTO__`) to an empty string. That makes rewritten body asset/chunk URLs (e.g. `<script src>`, `<link href>`) relative to the current document URL; if the extracted HTML is served from a subdirectory (e.g. `output.htmlFilename: "pages/[name].html"` with `publicPath: "auto"`), a hot update that patches `document.body.innerHTML` can rewrite URLs to resolve under that subdirectory and break resource loading.

Preserve the auto-public-path placeholder for the HMR DOM-patch variant and resolve it at runtime (e.g. replace it with `__webpack_require__.p` and add the publicPath runtime requirement) instead of stripping it at codegen time.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
This is the same concern as the (now-outdated) thread above, re-surfaced after the rebase — and the suggested fix unfortunately can't work on current `main`. `main`'s `JavascriptModulesPlugin.render` tap (added in #21018) resolves chunk-URL sentinels and then does `.split(CssUrlDependency.PUBLIC_PATH_AUTO).join("")` across the **entire** rendered JS chunk at build time. So:

1. Keeping the placeholder and emitting `…split("__WEBPACK_CSS_PUBLIC_PATH_AUTO__").join(__webpack_require__.p)` in the shim doesn't survive — the tap rewrites that literal `split(...)` argument to `split("")`, which shatters the HTML at runtime (verified: it splits the string into single characters). I tried exactly this and it broke.
2. The tap makes the JS export of an HTML module **deliberately root-relative** regardless of document location. The DOM-patch now reuses that same JS-export HTML, so it's consistent with `import html from "./page.html"` rather than diverging from it.

The residual subdirectory case (`output.htmlFilename: "pages/[name].html"` + `publicPath: "auto"`) is therefore a property of `main`'s JS-export URL resolution — the extracted `.html` asset uses an undo-path while the JS export is root-relative — not something this HMR PR introduces. Making the DOM-patch use undo-path semantics would mean changing that build-time mechanism in `HtmlModulesPlugin`/the render tap, which is out of scope here. Happy to open a follow-up for it if you'd like the JS export (and thus the DOM-patch) to honor the document's undo path.

body: The merge logic between per-call magic comments and `output.resourceHints` defaults doesn’t honor the documented precedence when the *other* hint is set by default. E.g. if `output.resourceHints.preload: true` and a call site specifies `/* webpackPrefetch: true */`, `dep.preload` will still be set from defaults, so preload wins and the per-call prefetch can’t override the project default. Consider treating `webpackPrefetch`/`webpackPreload` as a single mutually-exclusive choice: if either comment is present, ignore both defaults; otherwise apply defaults (with preload winning when both defaults are true).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `StartupAssetHintRuntimeModule` needs to run at the startup of *each chunk that contains URLDependencies with prefetch/preload hints* (including async chunks) to satisfy the “eagerly at chunk startup” behavior described in the changeset/PR. Currently it’s only added via `runtimeRequirementInTree`, which attaches it to the runtime chunk only; as a result, hinted `new URL(..., import.meta.url)` assets inside non-runtime/async chunks won’t trigger link injection before those chunk modules evaluate. Consider attaching this runtime module per-chunk (e.g. via `additionalChunkRuntimeRequirements` when the chunk runtime-requirements `set` contains `RuntimeGlobals.startupAssetHints`, or `runtimeRequirementInChunk.for(RuntimeGlobals.startupAssetHints)`).

body: `installed` is created as a plain object and queried via `installed[href]`. If `href` happens to equal an inherited key like "toString"/"constructor"/"__proto__", this can incorrectly short-circuit (or even mutate the prototype) and break hint injection/deduping. Use a prototype-less map (e.g. `Object.create(null)`) and/or guard lookups with a has-own-property check.

The fix ensures statically enumerable externals are present in the initial entry chunk so closure-bound external identifiers are available when the lazy activation update arrives. **Changes:** - Teach `LazyCompilationProxyModule` (inactive state) to add statically enumerable externals as dependencies when using closure-wrapped `output.library.type`. - Add hot test cases covering the reported UMD scenario (issue #19134) and a minimal UMD external reproduction. - Add additional hot test coverage for ESM-based externals types (`import`, `module`, `module-import`) under lazy compilation, plus a changeset entry. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 17 out of 17 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.

`collectStaticExternalRequests` treats any externals object as a request map and blindly reserves all `Object.keys(item)`. This will also reserve the special `byLayer` property (and potentially other non-request keys), causing a bogus dependency like `require('byLayer')` to be added and potentially turning it into an unexpected external in the library wrapper.

Consider excluding `byLayer` explicitly (and ideally resolving the correct layer first, similar to `ExternalModuleFactoryPlugin`’s `resolveLayer(...)`) before collecting request keys.

body: `collectStaticExternalRequests()` currently treats every key in an object-form `externals` as an external request. However `externals` object values can be boolean, and `false` is used to explicitly *disable* externalization for that key. Reserving dependencies for keys whose value is `false` would incorrectly pull those modules into the entry chunk and could change bundling behavior. Filter object keys by their value (e.g. skip when `item[request] === false`) before adding them to the reserved request set.

In that case the entry chunk will still get a UMD/AMD/System wrapper, but this branch won't run and the externals won’t be reserved, leaving the original runtime error unfixed for that configuration style. Consider checking `options.output.enabledLibraryTypes` (includes both output.library.type and per-entry library types) or otherwise accounting for entry-specific library types.

If a layer-specific map sets a request to `false` (i.e. disables externalization for that layer), the top-level key is still reserved, which can incorrectly pull an external (or even the real module) into the entry chunk for that layer. Consider resolving the effective externals map for the given layer first (similar to `resolveLayer` in `ExternalModuleFactoryPlugin`) and only reserving keys whose effective value is not `false`.

body: ## Pull request overview This PR optimizes `NormalModule#getSideEffectsConnectionState` by replacing the per-module generator allocation (introduced to avoid deep recursion stack overflows) with an explicit iterative traversal using parallel-array stacks. This keeps the walk stack-safe on deep chains while reducing allocations and improving rebuild performance. **Changes:** - Replaced the generator-trampoline side-effects walk with an allocation-light iterative loop using explicit stacks. - Added a fast-path helper to resolve side-effects state without descending when it can be determined from metadata / re-entry checks. - Extended unit tests with a branching (“diamond”) dependency case to validate correct aggregation across multiple deps. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 3 out of 3 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `lib/NormalModule.js` | Reworks side-effects traversal to an iterative stack-based algorithm with a metadata-based fast path. | | `test/NormalModule.unittest.js` | Adds a branching-deps test to validate correct state aggregation and flag cleanup. | | `.changeset/iterative-side-effects-walk.md` | Adds a patch changeset documenting the perf-focused rewrite. | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. body: `walkSideEffectsIterative` can encounter `ModuleGraphConnection.CIRCULAR_CONNECTION` (via `sideEffectsFastPath` when revisiting a module already on `modStack`), but it never sets `_sideEffectsCircularSeen`. Because `walkSideEffectsRecursive` uses `_sideEffectsCircularSeen` to decide whether it’s safe to memoize `_sideEffectsStateValue`, a cycle encountered in the iterative fallback can be missed and cause memoization of a cycle-tainted result. Consider marking `_sideEffectsCircularSeen = true` in the iterative walker whenever `state === ModuleGraphConnection.CIRCULAR_CONNECTION` (both in the pending-handling path and the main dep loop).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: In `walkSideEffectsIterative`, the non-`NormalModule` branch (`state = refModule.getSideEffectsConnectionState(moduleGraph)`) doesn’t mark the current walk as cycle-tainted. The recursive walker explicitly sets `_sideEffectsCircularSeen = true` before delegating in this scenario (see the comment in `walkSideEffectsRecursive`), because the delegated call can re-enter through `NormalModule.getSideEffectsConnectionState` and make caching decisions unsafe. Consider mirroring that behavior here so outer recursive frames don’t memoize results that depended on this re-entrant path.

This makes `true` results re-walk on subsequent lookups, even though `true` is safe to cache (and you already cache `true` in the direct `state === true` path below). Since `Compiler` keeps modules around across compilations and calls `module.cleanupForCache()` when clearing `_lastCompilation`, this field can prevent old compilations from being GC’d for modules that are not revisited to overwrite the slot. Consider clearing `_sideEffectsStateGraph`/`_sideEffectsStateValue` in `cleanupForCache()` (and/or `updateCacheModule()`), or switching to a weak-keyed structure (e.g. `WeakMap<ModuleGraph, ConnectionState>`) to avoid retaining `ModuleGraph` instances.

body: In the iterative walker, when a descended child returns `true` (handled via `pending`), the parent frame is popped without memoizing `_sideEffectsStateGraph/_sideEffectsStateValue`. Since `true` is monotonic (and the non-pending `state === true` path does memoize), this misses an easy cache hit and can cause repeated re-walks for modules whose result is `true` when the depth-limit fallback is used. body: `compiler.run` invokes its callback before `compiler.hooks.afterDone` is fired (see Compiler.run finalCallback). Clearing `stats.compilation.codeGenerationResults` inside the `run` callback can therefore break plugins tapping `afterDone` that expect compilation data to still be available. Defer `_releaseUnusedCompilationData` to the next tick (or otherwise after `afterDone`) to avoid changing hook semantics while still releasing memory quickly.

body: Changeset summary line is inconsistent with other changesets in this repo (they typically start with a capital letter and end with a period). Adjusting this keeps the generated changelog consistent.

body:
Good catch — the inline cleanup was running between the user's `run` callback and `compiler.hooks.afterDone.call(stats)` (Compiler.js:559–560), so any plugin tapping `afterDone` would have seen `codeGenerationResults` already cleared.

Fixed by moving the cleanup into a single `afterDone` tap registered in the `MultiCompiler` constructor with `stage: Infinity` (mirroring the `ProfilingPlugin` idiom at `lib/debug/ProfilingPlugin.js:319`). Tapable orders taps by stage, so ours runs **after** every other `afterDone` plugin has read the compilation — `afterDone` semantics are now preserved.

Deliberately avoided `process.nextTick`/`setImmediate` because webpack is moving toward browser-runtime compatibility; the tap-based approach needs no deferral primitive at all and works identically in Node and a browser.

`Compiler.close`'s path is unaffected: its cleanup runs inside the `cache.shutdown` async callback, which fires after `finalCallback` has already invoked `afterDone`.

Verified this is not timing-dependent — it passes deterministically (ran the case 8× in a row, plus it's green on every CI push). The reason is `MultiCompiler._runGraph`: when a child's `finalCallback` fires `nodeDone` (Compiler.js:559) followed immediately by `afterDone.call` (Compiler.js:560), `nodeDone` only *schedules* the completion worker via `process.nextTick` (`processQueue` → `MultiCompiler.js:586`). It never runs the worker synchronously.

So the ordering for the last-finishing child is:
1. `nodeDone(node)` → `processQueue()` schedules `processQueueWorker` on the next tick, returns
2. `afterDone.call(stats)` → our `stage: Infinity` tap clears `codeGenerationResults` (still synchronous, same stack)
3. `finalCallback` returns, stack unwinds
4. next tick → `processQueueWorker` sees all nodes done → builds `MultiStats` → invokes the user `run` callback → the assertions run

Because step 4 is deferred a tick past step 2, every child's `afterDone` (including the last) has already cleared its map by the time the callback runs. Leaving the test as-is.

body: `Compiler.close()` can clear `this._lastCompilation.codeGenerationResults.map` before `compiler.hooks.afterDone` has fired for the immediately preceding `compiler.run()` when `close()` is called from inside the `run` callback (a common pattern). Since `Compiler.run` invokes the user callback before `afterDone` (Compiler.js:559-560), synchronous `shutdown` taps can make this cleanup run before other `afterDone` observers, changing hook semantics and potentially breaking plugins that inspect codegen results in `afterDone`. Consider deferring this cleanup until after `afterDone` has executed (e.g., schedule the release in a microtask/macrotask when `close` is invoked during the `run` callback, or move the release into an `afterDone` tap gated by a flag set by `close`).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Good catch — confirmed real: `compiler.hooks.shutdown` has no default taps, so `callAsync` ran synchronously and the release fired before `afterDone` (Compiler.js:559→560) when `close()` is called from inside the `run` callback.

Fixed by deferring the release one microtask (`Promise.resolve().then(...)`): the synchronous `afterDone` at line 560 runs on the same stack right after the `run` callback returns, and microtasks only run once that stack unwinds, so `afterDone` taps still observe `codeGenerationResults` intact. Used a microtask rather than `process.nextTick`/`setImmediate` to keep the path browser-runtime compatible (consistent with the `MultiCompiler` afterDone-tap approach).

Updated the single-compiler regression test to call `close()` inside the `run` callback, assert via an `afterDone` tap that the map was still populated when `afterDone` fired, and assert the release + `stats.toJson()` afterwards. Verified it's a real guard — it fails when the release is made synchronous again.

author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Raw-text elements (e.g. `script`, `style`, `textarea`, `title`) are currently not pushed onto `openElements`, so any subsequent `text(...)` tokens will be appended to the *parent* (e.g. `<body>`) rather than becoming children of the raw-text element. This breaks the AST shape for common documents (e.g. `<script src=...>inline</script>`). Consider pushing raw-text elements onto the stack as normal, and ensure the `text` handler aggregates their content into a single text node under that element (and that tokenizer/tag parsing respects raw-text mode).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
When a closing tag matches an element below the top of the stack, `openElements.splice(i)` drops the matched element *and every element above it* without updating the `end` position for those implicitly-closed nodes. The resulting AST carries incorrect `end` offsets for those dropped nodes (often leaving them at the end of their start tag), which can break downstream tooling that relies on ranges. A tangible fix is to set `end` for all popped elements in `openElements[i..]` before splicing them off (same applies to other implicit closes like the `

` logic).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `buildHtmlAst(source)` performs a full token walk via `walkHtmlTokens`, but `HtmlParser` already tokenizes/walks HTML as part of parsing. Doing a second full pass for every HTML module can measurably increase parse time and memory churn on large builds. Consider reusing the existing tokenization results / integrating AST construction into the existing parsing pass, or gating AST creation behind an option/feature flag so it’s only computed when needed.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: This introduces a new public-ish field (`buildInfo.htmlAst`) but the accompanying type declarations don’t appear to be updated in this PR (the only `types.d.ts` change is unrelated). To keep TypeScript consumers and internal tooling type-safe, add `htmlAst` to the `BuildInfo` type (and export/define the AST node shapes if they’re meant to be consumed).

body:

` should close `span` with a sensible `end`). This will prevent regressions in tree-shape and source-range correctness.

Add a guard inside the loop (e.g., `if (!dep) continue;`) to preserve the prior safety behavior while still processing all valid deps.

body: `hasLibrary` treats `library: false` as 'present' because it only checks `!== undefined`. In webpack configs, `output.library` (or `entrypoint.options.library`) can be explicitly set to `false` to disable library output, which should not trigger `useSharedEntryExports`. Consider checking `library != null && library !== false` (and apply the same condition to the later `hasLibrary` calculation in this file) so the shared/merged exports logic only runs for actual library output.

body: Merging exports via `for (var ... in __webpack_entry_exports__)` copies inherited enumerable properties too. That can lead to unexpected keys (and is generally unsafe if the export object has a polluted prototype). Prefer iterating over own properties only (e.g., `Object.keys(...)` or an `hasOwnProperty` guard). Also consider guarding for non-object return values (e.g., primitives) before iterating, since the current `if(__webpack_entry_exports__)` check will treat strings/numbers as truthy and can produce unintended merged keys.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The `hasLibrary` detection logic is duplicated in this file (once for `useSharedEntryExports` and again for `mergeEntryExports`). To reduce the risk of diverging behavior over time (and to ensure fixes like properly handling `library: false` are applied consistently), consider extracting this into a small local helper (e.g., `const entrypointHasLibrary = (entrypoint) => ...`) and reuse it in both places.

body:
Deliberate — `buildInfo.hash` alone is insufficient here. The inline CSS body itself is part of the HTML source (already covered by the HTML module's own `buildInfo.hash`); what we need to propagate is changes that ride on the CSS module's *dependencies* — e.g. an asset referenced via `url(...)` whose bytes changed. That kind of change moves the CSS module's *module* hash via `CssUrlDependency.updateHash`, not its `buildInfo.hash`. The watch test `html-contenthash-inline-style-url` covers exactly this: with `buildInfo.hash` only (commit 3b2574e) the HTML's `[contenthash]` stayed pinned across the asset swap; recursing into `module.updateHash` is what makes it invalidate. Scope is bounded — the inline-CSS module's dep chain is the inline body + `url(...)` references, not the full app graph.

In the JS-export path (`undoPath` is a string), unresolved `__WEBPACK_HTML_CHUNK_URL__…` sentinels can leak into the exported HTML string when chunk filename templates contain placeholders that are only available after `Compilation#createHash()` (e.g. `[contenthash]`). Because the JS export is emitted as a single JSON-stringified literal, there is no later pass that can resolve these sentinels, so consumers importing the HTML module can observe broken `src`/`href` values.

Consider ensuring JS exports never contain chunk-URL sentinels: e.g. generate chunk URLs via runtime expressions (string concatenation using runtime chunk-url helpers), or defer JS-source generation until after chunk hashes exist (similar to the extracted-HTML renderManifest phase) so `compilation.getPath()` can resolve `[contenthash]`.

body:
This step asserts the JS bundle’s `[contenthash]` changes when the referenced asset URL changes, but `1/index.js` → `2/index.js` also changes the JS source itself (extra requires/tests). That can make the `[contenthash]` change even if the asset URL did not participate in hashing, so the regression signal is weakened.

To make this a reliable guard, keep the entry JS source identical between steps 1 and 2 (change only `logo.png`) and use `STATE` to vary expectations across rebuilds.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Good catch — fixed in a follow-up commit. `HtmlGenerator._renderHtml`'s JS-export path now intentionally leaves chunk-URL sentinels in place, and `HtmlModulesPlugin` runs a global `processAssets` pass at `PROCESS_ASSETS_STAGE_OPTIMIZE_INLINE` that resolves them across every asset (most importantly the JS chunk holding `module.exports = "<html>"`). Because that stage runs after `createHash()` populates chunk hashes — and before `realContentHash`'s `OPTIMIZE_HASH` stage — chunk filenames with `[contenthash]` / `[chunkhash]` / `[fullhash]` resolve correctly, and `realContentHash` rehashes the JS chunk's own filename against the resolved bytes. Asserted by a new step-0 assertion in `html-contenthash-inline-script/0/index.js` that reads `main.<hash>.js` from disk and verifies no `__WEBPACK_HTML_CHUNK_URL__<hex>__<type>__END__` sentinels survive.

Acknowledged but intentionally not changing: this test is a regression guard for already-working `URLDependency` behavior (it predates this PR's HTML changes), not a verification of the HTML fix. The watchCases harness bundles each step's `index.js` source into the JS bundle whose contenthash we're measuring, so step-1 and step-2 assertions can't share source without resorting to a `WATCH_STEP` dispatch in a single shared file — awkward enough that I'd rather accept the weakened signal here. body:
Reworked in abaaf97 per request — the sentinel resolution now taps `JavascriptModulesPlugin.getCompilationHooks(compilation).render` instead of `processAssets`. This runs during `createChunkAssets`, after `createHash` populates chunk content hashes, so every later consumer of the chunk asset (`SourceMapDevToolPlugin` at stage 500, size optimizers at stage 400, `RealContentHashPlugin` at 2500, compression, etc.) reads the already-resolved bytes. The per-chunk `RawSource` identity cache survives the move and keeps warm rebuilds stable against `getLazyHashedEtag`'s WeakMap.

## Pull request overview Improves watch-mode incremental rebuild performance for large context dependencies by avoiding overly broad filesystem cache invalidation and reducing per-file hook-call overhead in context dependency resolution. **Changes:** - Narrow `inputFileSystem.purge()` invalidation for explicitly watched context directories by using `{ exact: true }` (enhanced-resolve ≥ 5.22.0) in `NodeWatchFileSystem`. - Batch `ContextModuleFactory`’s `alternativeRequests` hook invocation per directory instead of per file to reduce overhead. - Bump `enhanced-resolve` to `^5.22.0` and update filesystem purge typings/documentation accordingly; add changesets for release notes. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 6 out of 8 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `lib/node/NodeWatchFileSystem.js` | Purges watched directories with `{ exact: true }` to avoid wiping descendant stat cache on directory change events. | | `lib/ContextModuleFactory.js` | Batches `alternativeRequests` hook calls per directory to cut per-file overhead during context resolution. | | `lib/util/fs.js` | Extends `Purge` typedef docs/signature to include optional `{ exact?: boolean }`. | | `package.json` | Updates `enhanced-resolve` dependency to `^5.22.0`. | | `yarn.lock` | Locks `enhanced-resolve` to `5.22.0`. | | `types.d.ts` | Updates generated `InputFileSystem.purge` type signature to accept optional `{ exact?: boolean }`. | | `.changeset/watch-narrow-purge-context-dir.md` | Adds changeset entry for the watch-mode purge narrowing performance fix. | | `.changeset/batch-alternative-requests.md` | Adds changeset entry for batching `alternativeRequests` performance improvement. |

## Pull request overview Adds a new HTML-module parser option (`module.parser.html.sources`) to control whether/which URL-like HTML attributes are extracted as webpack dependencies/entries, enabling full opt-out or customization. **Changes:** - Implement `module.parser.html.sources` in `HtmlParser` (supports `true`/`false`/custom array; adds new `stylesheet-inline` handling). - Wire defaults, schema validation, and public typings for the new parser option. - Add configCases coverage for `sources: false`, opt-out-by-array, and custom source types; update defaults snapshots accordingly. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 32 out of 37 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | lib/html/HtmlParser.js | Core implementation of the `sources` option and new source-type handling. | | lib/html/HtmlModulesPlugin.js | Validates `module.parser.html` options against the new schema; passes options into `HtmlParser`. | | lib/config/defaults.js | Sets default `module.parser.html.sources` to `true` when `experiments.html` is enabled. | | schemas/WebpackOptions.json | Adds `HtmlParserOptions` schema and exposes it under `module.parser.html`. | | schemas/plugins/HtmlParserOptions.json | Plugin schema ref for `HtmlParserOptions`. | | schemas/plugins/HtmlParserOptions.check.js | Generated schema validator for `HtmlParserOptions`. | | schemas/plugins/HtmlParserOptions.check.d.ts | Generated TS typings for the validator module. | | declarations/WebpackOptions.d.ts | Public TypeScript typings for `module.parser.html.sources`. | | types.d.ts | Generated bundled typings updated for the new option/types. | | lib/dependencies/HtmlScriptSrcDependency.js | Aligns `elementKind` naming (`script` vs `script-classic`). | | test/configCases/html/parser-sources-disabled/* | Tests that `sources: false` leaves URL attributes untouched but still processes inline `
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `tagSources`/`sourceItem` are looked up via plain-object property access (`this.sourcesByTag[elementName]` and `tagSources[attributeName]`). Because these tables have `Object.prototype` in their prototype chain, HTML can trigger inherited/special keys (e.g. an attribute named `__proto__`, `constructor`, `toString`, etc.) and `sourceItem` becomes a non-SourceItem value, which then falls through and can incorrectly create `HtmlScriptSrcDependency` entries. This also means `sources: false` can be bypassed for special tag names like `__proto__`. Use a `Map` (like before) or null-prototype objects (`Object.create(null)`) plus an own-property check (e.g. `hasOwnProperty.call(...)`) for both the tag and attribute lookups.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `stylesheet-inline` builds a non-base64 `data:text/css,...` request using `encodeURIComponent`. `decodeDataURI` decodes non-base64 bodies with `Buffer.from(decoded, "ascii")`, which can corrupt non-ASCII CSS. Consider mirroring the inline-`<script>` approach in this file and base64-encoding the CSS content (or otherwise ensuring UTF-8 round-tripping) so Unicode in inline CSS attributes is handled correctly.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: This file now uses entry group names `script` / `script-module` (see updated `EntryScriptInfo` type), but there are still comments later in the file referring to the old `classic` / `esm-script` terminology. Updating those comments would help keep the documentation consistent with the current group keys.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Fixed in 38dd497. The lookup tables (`DEFAULT_SOURCES_BY_TAG`, the per-tag buckets, the any-tag table, and the empty `sources: false` table) are now built via a `dict()` helper that returns `Object.create(null)`, so tag/attribute names like `constructor`/`__proto__`/`toString` no longer resolve to inherited values. Added a regression test in `parser-sources-disabled` with `<constructor name="./proto-bypass.js">` that would have created a bogus entry (and failed the build) under the old plain-object tables.

body:
Fixed in 38dd497. Both inline-CSS paths (the `<style>` body and the `stylesheet-inline` attribute) now build `data:text/css;base64,<utf8-base64>` instead of `data:text/css,${encodeURIComponent(...)}`, mirroring the inline-`<script>` handler. This avoids the `Buffer.from(decoded, "ascii")` truncation in `decodeDataURI` for non-ASCII CSS. The `style-tag` test now asserts a non-ASCII `content: "café →"` rule round-trips intact.

body: For user-defined `sources` entries with `type: "script"` / `"script-module"` on non-`<script>` tags (e.g. `<my-script src>`), this passes the custom element’s `tagStart`/`tagOpenEnd` into `HtmlScriptSrcDependency`. The dependency template assumes the originating tag is `<script>`/`<link>` and will clone `originalTag` to emit sibling tags for runtime/split chunks, including appending `</script>` for script kinds. With a custom tag this can generate invalid HTML and/or fail to load required sibling chunks. Consider either (a) teaching `HtmlScriptSrcDependency.Template` to synthesize real `<script>`/`<link>` siblings when `originalTag` isn’t a matching native tag, or (b) not providing `tagStart`/`tagOpenEnd` for custom tags until sibling emission is supported. A regression test with splitChunks/runtimeChunk for a custom `script` source would help lock this down.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Implemented option (a) in c63d2a4. `HtmlScriptSrcDependency.Template` now detects whether the originating tag is the native element for its kind (`<script>` for `script`/`script-module`, `<link>` for `stylesheet`/`modulepreload`) via `isNativeTagForKind`. Native tags are still cloned verbatim (preserving `defer`/`async`/`media`/etc.); for a custom element mapped to a `script`/`script-module`/`stylesheet` `type`, the template now synthesizes a real `<script>`/`<link rel="stylesheet">` sibling (new `buildScriptTag`, reusing the existing `buildStylesheetLink`), copying the CSP/fetch attributes (`nonce`/`crossorigin`/`referrerpolicy`). The custom element's own tag is still rewritten in place to its entry chunk, which is the intended behavior for a user-declared source.

Added `test/configCases/html/parser-sources-custom-tag-siblings` — a custom `<my-script>` (`script`) and `<my-module>` (`script-module`) with `optimization.runtimeChunk`, asserting the split-out runtime chunk is loaded by a real classic / `type="module"` `<script>` sibling and that no `<my-script …></script>` / `<my-module …></script>` clone is emitted. Existing native-tag sibling tests (extract-runtime-chunk, extract-split-chunks, css-runtime-and-split-chunks) still pass unchanged.

body: When `output.module` is enabled and a user maps a *custom* element to `type: "script"`, `willBeModuleScript` stays false unless the originating tag is literally `<script>`. That leaves `elementKind` as `"script"`, so `HtmlScriptSrcDependency.Template` will synthesize classic `<script src=...>` siblings (no `type="module"`) for runtime/split chunks. Those siblings will try to load ESM output as classic scripts and will fail at runtime. Consider deriving `elementKind` for `type: "script"` from `outputModule` regardless of `elementName` (while still only mutating the original tag’s `type` attribute when `elementName === "script"`).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Fixed in efcd2b9. `willBeModuleScript` no longer requires `elementName === "script"`, so under `output.module` any `type: "script"` source — native `<script>` or a custom element — resolves to `elementKind: "script-module"`. The template therefore synthesizes `<script type="module">` siblings for the ESM runtime/split chunks instead of classic scripts that would fail to load an ES module. The in-place `type`-attribute rewrite (`reconcileScriptTypeAttr`) stays guarded by `elementName === "script"`, so a custom element's own attributes are left untouched.

Added `test/configCases/html/parser-sources-custom-tag-module-siblings` — a custom `<my-script>` (`type: script`) with `experiments.outputModule` + `runtimeChunk`, asserting the runtime sibling is emitted as `<script type="module" src="…">` and that no classic sibling or cloned `<my-script …></script>` appears.

It also adds a regression test case and includes a small comment correction in the JS parser. **Changes:** - Include `runtimeCondition` for `info.type === "external"` entries in `ConcatenatedModule#updateHash`. - Add a `configCases` regression test that asserts the concatenated module hash changes when only an external-info `runtimeCondition` changes. - Update a TODO comment in `JavascriptParser` to reference import phases (not assertions) and add a changeset entry. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 7 out of 7 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `lib/optimize/ConcatenatedModule.js` | Hash now incorporates external-info `runtimeCondition` so runtime-conditional externals affect cache invalidation. | | `test/configCases/concatenate-modules/external-runtime-condition-hash/webpack.config.js` | New config-case plugin asserts hash differs when external runtimeCondition differs. | | `test/configCases/concatenate-modules/external-runtime-condition-hash/index.js` | Test entry verifies compilation works with an external excluded from concatenation. | | `test/configCases/concatenate-modules/external-runtime-condition-hash/lib.js` | Creates an external import/re-export to force an external info entry in concatenation list. | | `test/configCases/concatenate-modules/external-runtime-condition-hash/errors.js` | Declares no expected errors for the new config-case. | | `lib/javascript/JavascriptParser.js` | Comment-only TODO clarification (import phases). | | `.changeset/concatenated-module-hash-runtime-condition.md` | Records the patch-level change for release notes. |

body: In the new external-info hashing, `moduleId` and `runtimeConditionToString(...)` are appended back-to-back with no delimiter/prefix. Because both are variable-length strings, different (moduleId, runtimeCondition) pairs can produce identical byte streams (e.g. "ab"+"c" vs "a"+"bc"), increasing the chance of deterministic hash collisions. Consider adding an explicit separator/prefix (e.g. "|runtimeCondition=") when updating the hash so the structure is unambiguous.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Good catch — fixed in `ba077ce` by combining moduleId and runtimeCondition into a single `hash.update` with a `|` delimiter, so the two segments can no longer slide across each other in the streaming hash.

body: `ConcatenatedModule#updateHash` now includes `runtimeCondition` for external infos, but it still ignores `info.nonDeferAccess` (and whether the external module is deferred via `moduleGraph.isDeferred(info.module)`). Both values affect the emitted code for externals (e.g. the extra “non-deferred import to a deferred module” path), so changing them can currently leave the module hash unchanged and allow persistent-cache reuse of stale output. Consider incorporating `nonDeferAccess` and the deferred flag into the external-info hash update as well.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Good point — verified both fields actually drive code generation:

- `info.deferred` (set from `moduleGraph.isDeferred(info.module)`) gates the deferred-external branch in `renderConcatenatedModule` (`if (info.type === "external" && info.deferred)`, `if (!info.deferred)`)
- `info.nonDeferAccess` together with `info.deferred` triggers the extra non-defer-access path (`if (info.deferred && nonDeferAccess)`)

Both can change without the module id changing (e.g. when a different consumer adds/removes an `import.defer`), so the same persistent-cache staleness applies. Folded both into the hash update in `4ab1bf8` — same single `hash.update` with `|`-delimited segments.

body: The Summary guidance is framed as "what's broken" + "the fix", but this PR template is used for non-fix changes too (feat/docs/refactor/etc). This wording can mislead authors of those PRs; consider using a more general phrasing like "motivation/problem" + "what changed" instead of assuming something was broken.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: This suggests putting longer explanations in the commit message, but webpack PRs are often squashed (see CONTRIBUTING.md guidance and this doc’s own mention of an eventual squash commit). With squash merges, detailed context in individual commit messages is easy to lose or not surface to reviewers; consider directing extended context to the PR discussion (a comment/thread) or a linked issue instead of commit messages.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Good catch — "what's broken + the fix" was fix-PR-shaped and would have misled `feat`/`refactor`/`perf`/`docs` authors. Reworded to "one sentence on motivation (what problem / use case / gap) and one on what changed", with an explicit note that the "broken/fixed" framing only fits `fix` PRs and other change types should describe the missing capability or rationale instead.
