body:
Fair point — webpack squash-merges, so per-commit messages on the feature branch get folded into a single squash commit whose body is inherited from the PR body. Pointing readers at "the commit message" was misleading. Reworded the brevity rule to redirect long context to (a) inline review threads, (b) a linked discussion issue, or (c) the squash-merge commit body via the PR description itself — with the explicit note that the PR body is what *becomes* that commit body on merge, so keeping it short is still the right call.

body: The merge currently only mutates `entrypoint.options`, but the `ChunkGroupInfo` fields derived from these options (`cgi.runtime`, `cgi.chunkLoading`, `cgi.asyncChunks`) stay as they were when the entrypoint was first created. That means merged options (e.g. `asyncChunks: false` or a previously-undefined `runtime`) may not actually affect chunk graph building beyond the `entrypoint.options` object. Consider updating the corresponding `cgi.*` fields when copying these specific options (or recomputing them from the merged options) so the merged entryOptions take effect during traversal.

To match other compilation errors (e.g. `AsyncDependencyToInitialChunkError`), consider setting `err.module = module` and `err.loc = b.loc` (or using a small dedicated error class) so users can trace the conflict back to the originating source location.

body:
Good catch — this is the key reason the implementation was reworked. Syncing the derived `cgi.runtime/chunkLoading/asyncChunks` mid-build turned out to be unsafe: an entrypoint can be joined after its own modules were already traversed, and recomputing the fallback values against the joining block's parent (or mutating `cgi.runtime`) changed traversal results — in practice it sent `web-worker` into a hang.

So this PR deliberately scopes the change to gap-filling `entrypoint.options` only, and does not touch the `ChunkGroupInfo` traversal state. The merged options are recorded on the shared entrypoint but, for the structural keys, the values chosen when the entrypoint was first created remain authoritative for traversal. Making those take effect safely would need a larger redesign of how/when entrypoint structure is finalized, which is out of scope here.

body:
Resolved — the conflict `WebpackError` was removed altogether. Blocks that dedupe to one entrypoint (e.g. multiple workers pointing at the same module) legitimately carry differing options such as an auto-generated per-worker `runtime`, so treating a difference as a conflict produced false positives (it regressed the `web-worker` case). The path now only gap-fills missing options and never emits an error, so there's no longer an error needing `module`/`loc` attribution.

body: The new gap-fill merge copies keys from `entryOptions` onto `entrypoint.options` using bracket assignment. Since `entryOptions` can originate from magic comments (e.g. `webpackEntryOptions` in WorkerPlugin), this allows special keys like `__proto__`, `constructor`, or `prototype` to be copied and potentially mutate the target object's prototype (prototype-pollution style issue). Please explicitly skip these keys (and/or use a null-prototype options object) before assigning into `existingOptions`.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Good catch — fixed in 5fbee11. The gap-fill now skips `__proto__`, `constructor` and `prototype` before assigning onto `entrypoint.options`, since `entryOptions` can come from the `webpackEntryOptions` magic comment in user source.

Note: `__proto__`/`constructor` were already incidentally skipped by the `existingOptions[key] !== undefined` read-guard (both resolve on the prototype chain), but `prototype` was genuinely writable (plain objects have no inherited `prototype`), so the explicit denylist is the real fix rather than relying on that coincidence. The `entry-options-merge` test now passes a second worker carrying `prototype`/`constructor` keys and asserts they are not copied onto the entrypoint options and the global prototype is untouched.

body:
Intentionally not duplicating this guard in `buildChunkGraph`. The sanitization is done once at the trust boundary where the untrusted `webpackEntryOptions` magic comment is consumed (`WorkerPlugin`, commit 8ad7622). By the time `entryOptions` reaches this gap-fill it has already been sanitized — the only producers of an async block's `entryOptions` are `WorkerPlugin` (now guarded) and `LoaderPlugin` (which builds `entryOptions` internally, not from user input). Keeping the check in one place avoids drift between two denylists and keeps `buildChunkGraph` free of input-validation concerns. The `entry-options-merge` test exercises the end-to-end path (`prototype`/`constructor`/`__proto__` in a worker's magic comment never reach `entrypoint.options`).

body: ## Pull request overview This PR optimizes `Compilation.renameAsset` and `Compilation.deleteAsset` by avoiding per-call scans over all chunks. It introduces a lazily built reverse index from asset filename → chunks, intended to significantly reduce runtime when many assets are renamed/deleted. **Changes:** - Add lazy reverse indices (`file -> Set`) for `chunk.files` and `chunk.auxiliaryFiles`, and invalidate them on `unseal()` / `clearAssets()`. - Update `renameAsset`/`deleteAsset` to use the reverse indices instead of iterating all chunks. - Add a patch changeset documenting the performance improvement. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | lib/Compilation.js | Adds and uses lazy reverse indices to accelerate `deleteAsset`/`renameAsset`, plus invalidation logic. | | .changeset/compilation-asset-chunk-reverse-index.md | Documents the perf change as a patch release note. | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: _renameAssetInIndex unconditionally adds `newFile` to `chunk.files`/`chunk.auxiliaryFiles` for every chunk listed in the index. If the reverse index is stale (e.g. chunk file sets were mutated after the index was built), this can introduce incorrect extra entries because `Set#delete` might return false. Consider only adding `newFile` when the old name was actually present (e.g. check the return value of `delete` or compare sizes, like the pre-index implementation did).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The reverse index is cached after the first delete/rename, but it is not kept in sync when chunk file sets are later populated (e.g. `createChunkAssets` adds to `chunk.files`/`chunk.auxiliaryFiles`, and `createModuleAssets` adds to `chunk.auxiliaryFiles`). If a plugin calls `renameAsset`/`deleteAsset` earlier in the seal pipeline (e.g. from the `moduleAsset` hook), the index can be built while `chunk.files` is still empty and will then miss subsequently generated chunk assets; later `deleteAsset`/`renameAsset` calls won’t clean up `chunk.files`/`chunk.auxiliaryFiles`. Either delay building until after chunk assets are generated, or update the index when these sets are mutated.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Good catch — fixed in 78284ba. `emitAsset()` now invalidates the reverse index. Every internal `chunk.files` / `chunk.auxiliaryFiles` addition (`createModuleAssets`, `createChunkAssets`, `SourceMapDevToolPlugin`, `HotModuleReplacementPlugin`) is immediately preceded by an `emitAsset` for that exact file, so dropping the cache there guarantees the next `deleteAsset`/`renameAsset` rebuilds from the live chunk sets and can never miss a late-added entry — including the `moduleAsset`-hook-before-`createChunkAssets` case you described. In the normal pipeline all emits happen before the `processAssets` delete/rename batch, so the index still builds once and the perf win is preserved. Added a unit test (`test/Compilation.unittest.js`) that builds the index early, emits + attaches an asset afterwards, then deletes/renames it; it fails without the `emitAsset` invalidation.

body:
Fixed in 78284ba. `_renameAssetInIndex` now guards on `Set#delete` and only adds `newFile` (and records the chunk under `newFile` in the index) for chunks where the old name was actually present — restoring the pre-index behavior. Combined with the `emitAsset` invalidation (see the other thread), the index is no longer cached while stale, but the per-chunk guard keeps the method robust regardless.

body: `emitAsset()` only invalidates `_assetToChunkIndex`/`_assetToChunkAuxiliaryIndex` in the “new asset” path. When `emitAsset()` hits an early-return path (asset already exists with same content, or conflict/update paths), callers can still add the file to `chunk.files`/`chunk.auxiliaryFiles` afterward. This happens internally in `createChunkAssets` (it can emit the same filename for multiple chunks via `alreadyWrittenFiles`, then does `chunk.(auxiliary)Files.add(file)` after `emitAsset`). If the reverse index was built earlier, it can become stale and later `deleteAsset`/`renameAsset` may miss chunks that received the file after an early-return emit. Consider invalidating the reverse index at the start of `emitAsset()` (or in every return path), not only when `this.assets[file]` is initially absent. Adding a unit test for the “existing asset emitted again + chunk.files.add” case would prevent regressions.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Good catch — fixed in f28b5fa. You're right that `createChunkAssets` re-emits a shared filename for each chunk (the `alreadyWritten` path at lines 5387-5398, hitting `emitAsset`'s same-content early-return) and then calls `chunk.files.add(file)`, so the new-asset-only invalidation could leave the index stale for the second chunk. I moved the invalidation to the **start** of `emitAsset` so every return path (new, same-content re-emit, and conflict) drops the cache; the next `deleteAsset`/`renameAsset` then rebuilds from the live chunk sets. Added a unit test (`deleteAsset cleans every chunk for an asset shared via re-emit`) that builds the index after the first chunk, re-emits the shared asset for a second chunk, and asserts both chunks are cleaned — it fails with the previous new-asset-only invalidation. Perf is unaffected: invalidation is O(1) and in the normal pipeline all emits still precede the processAssets delete/rename batch, so the index is built once.

body: The reverse index can become incomplete if `chunk.files`/`chunk.auxiliaryFiles` are mutated directly after the index is built (these sets are part of the public API and can be modified by plugins). In that case `index.get(file)` will be `undefined` and `deleteAsset` will skip removing `file` from chunks, leaving stale chunk file entries. Consider a correctness fallback when the index has no entry (e.g. scan all chunks and delete from the sets, and/or rebuild the index) so `deleteAsset` still cleans up even when the cached index is missing newly-added references.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `_renameAssetInIndex` bails out when `index.get(file)` is `undefined`. If the cached index is missing newly-added references (e.g. a plugin adds an existing asset filename to `chunk.files` after the index was built), `renameAsset` will not rename that chunk entry, changing behavior vs the previous full-scan implementation. Consider handling the “index missing entries” case (e.g. rebuild or do a one-time scan fallback) to preserve correctness when chunk file sets are mutated outside of `emitAsset`-adjacent code paths.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Addressed in ed6b6a5. `deleteAsset` now falls back to scanning all chunks when `file` is absent from **both** the `files` and `auxiliaryFiles` reverse indexes — i.e. it isn't tracked anywhere, which is the case where a direct `chunk.files`/`chunk.auxiliaryFiles` mutation (bypassing `emitAsset`) could otherwise be missed. This restores the pre-index cleanup behavior. The fast path is kept whenever the file is present in either index, so the common bulk-delete case stays O(matching chunks); the scan only runs for assets not tracked in any chunk, which is the same complexity the original full-scan code had. Added a unit test (`deleteAsset falls back to scanning when the file is not in the index`) that builds the index, then attaches an already-emitted asset directly to a chunk and asserts deleteAsset still removes it (fails without the fallback).

Note I gate the fallback on *both* indexes being empty rather than per-side, because a normal asset lives in `files` xor `auxiliaryFiles`; a per-side miss-scan would scan on essentially every call and defeat the optimization.

body:
Update: in 3e771ea I reverted the secondary-scan approach. Benchmarking showed that sweeping the remaining chunks on every call returns `deleteAsset` to O(assets × chunks) — it measured ~20–60% **slower** than the original full-scan (e.g. 8000 chunks: full-scan ~1900 ms, hybrid ~2390 ms), which defeats the whole point of this PR (the indexed fast path is ~5.5 ms there).

So `deleteAsset` keeps the indexed fast path (O(matching chunks)) plus the scan fallback for files absent from **both** indexes. The "index incomplete for an already-indexed file" case is handled by contract rather than by scanning: `emitAsset` invalidates the reverse index, and every internal `chunk.files`/`chunk.auxiliaryFiles` mutation in webpack core (`createModuleAssets`, `createChunkAssets`, `SourceMapDevToolPlugin`, `HotModuleReplacementPlugin`) is paired with an `emitAsset` for that file — so the indexed sets are always complete for assets registered through `emitAsset`. The only uncovered path is a plugin raw-adding an already-emitted filename to another chunk's set without calling `emitAsset`, which is outside the asset-registration contract.

Update: reverted the secondary scan in 3e771ea. Sweeping the non-indexed chunks on every `renameAsset` made it O(assets × chunks) again — benchmarked ~20–60% slower than the original full-scan, which negates this PR's purpose (the indexed fast path is two-to-three orders of magnitude faster on many-chunk builds).

`renameAsset` keeps the indexed fast path via `_renameAssetInChunk` (which guards on `Set#delete`), plus the scan fallback when the file is in neither index. The already-indexed-but-incomplete case is handled by contract: `emitAsset` invalidates the reverse index and every internal `chunk.files`/`auxiliaryFiles` mutation in core is paired with an `emitAsset`, so the indexed sets are complete for assets registered through `emitAsset`. A plugin raw-adding an already-emitted filename to another chunk without `emitAsset` is outside that contract.

Use a more resilient pattern (like the one in `test/configCases/html/css-imported-from-js/test.js`) that matches `src`/`href` anywhere within the tag.

body: ## Pull request overview This PR fixes a `DefinePlugin` behavior where accessing an undefined property on an object definition could inline the entire object (potentially leaking secrets), by resolving unknown member-chain accesses to `undefined` via `JavascriptParser`’s `expressionMemberChain`/`callMemberChain` hooks (issue #15559). **Changes:** - Add `expressionMemberChain`/`callMemberChain` handling in `DefinePlugin` to substitute unknown object-member accesses with `undefined` (and avoid inlining the whole object). - Add config-case coverage for unknown member access (including dotted keys, optional chaining, and calls). - Add a changeset entry for a patch release note. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 4 out of 4 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `lib/DefinePlugin.js` | Implements undefined-member-chain handling for object definitions and refactors object-definition detection. | | `test/configCases/plugins/define-plugin/webpack.config.js` | Extends DefinePlugin test configuration with nested and dotted object keys to reproduce the issue. | | `test/configCases/plugins/define-plugin/index.js` | Adds assertions covering unknown member access, optional chaining, and call-chain behavior. | | `.changeset/define-plugin-undefined-member.md` | Documents the behavioral fix as a patch change. | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `isUndefinedMemberAccess` uses the `in` operator (`member in value` / `nextPath in definitions`). This treats inherited/prototype properties (e.g. `constructor`, `toString`, `__proto__`) as “defined”, which can skip the `undefined` substitution and re-enable object inlining/leakage. Use an own-property check instead (e.g. `Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(...)`) for both the object definition and the `definitions` map.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The hook always records `addValueDependency(key)` for the object-define key that registered the handler. For chains that resolve through a dotted sibling key (e.g. resolving `OBJECT.SUB2.b` via `definitions["OBJECT.SUB2"]` inside the `OBJECT` handler), this can miss value-dependencies on the sibling key and lead to stale filesystem-cache invalidation when `OBJECT.SUB2` changes. Consider tracking and adding value dependencies for any `nextPath` consulted, or ensure the most specific dotted-key handler runs first (e.g. via hook stage/ordering).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Same value-dependency issue applies to `callMemberChain`: when the undefined detection succeeds due to a dotted sibling key (e.g. `OBJECT.SUB2`), `addValueDependency(key)` may only record the parent object key even though the outcome depends on the sibling definition. This can cause incorrect cache invalidation when the dotted definition changes.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
For the `definitions`-map lookup I switched to `Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(...)` as suggested.

For `member in value` I kept the `in` operator on purpose: a define like `OBJECT: { SUB: 1 }` means `OBJECT` *is* that object literal, so inherited members must keep working — `OBJECT.toString()` should evaluate to the object's `toString`, not `undefined`. Switching to an own-property check would rewrite `OBJECT.toString`/`OBJECT.hasOwnProperty` to `undefined` and break those calls. Genuinely-absent properties (typos, missing secrets) are not on `Object.prototype`, so `in` already returns `false` for them and the object is not inlined. Accessing an actual inherited member inherently requires materializing the object, so there's no extra leak beyond what that access implies.

---

body:
Good catch. `isUndefinedMemberAccess` now collects every define key it consults (including dotted siblings resolved via `definitions[nextPath]`), and both the `expressionMemberChain` and `callMemberChain` handlers call `addValueDependency` for each before substituting — so changing `OBJECT.SUB2` invalidates modules whose output depended on it. Fixed in 372f6e2 (covers the `callMemberChain` case in the sibling comment too).

body: `isUndefinedMemberAccess` uses the `in` operator (`member in value`) when checking whether a member exists on a defined object. This treats inherited/prototype properties (e.g. `toString`, `constructor`, `__proto__`) as “defined”, so accesses like `OBJECT.toString` won’t be rewritten to `undefined` and can still trigger full object inlining/leakage. Consider restricting the check to own-properties (e.g. via `Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call`) to ensure only explicitly defined keys are treated as present and prototype chain properties don’t bypass the protection.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The dotted-sibling lookup (`else if (nextPath in definitions)`) only checks `definitions` from the current `DefinePlugin` instance. If another `DefinePlugin` (or plugin using `DefinePlugin.getCompilationHooks(compilation).definitions`) defines `OBJECT.SUB2`, this instance may still treat `OBJECT.SUB2.*` as missing and rewrite the whole chain to `undefined`, preventing the parser’s fallback from applying the other definition. Consider basing the sibling lookup on the merged compilation-wide definitions (from the `definitions` hook) so member-chain resolution works correctly across multiple DefinePlugin instances.

body: `CallExpression` typedef was added but isn’t referenced anywhere in this file. Consider removing it to avoid unused/leftover type declarations (or use it where intended).

body: The JSDoc for `isObjectDefinition` says it only matches “plain object/array”, but the predicate currently accepts any non-null object except `RuntimeValue` and `RegExp` (e.g. Date/Map instances would also match). Either tighten the predicate to match the doc, or adjust the doc to reflect the actual behavior.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Keeping `in` here intentionally. A define like `OBJECT: { SUB: 1 }` means `OBJECT` *is* that object literal, so inherited members must keep working: today `OBJECT.SUB.toString()` compiles to `({"a":1}).toString()` and returns `"[object Object]"`. Switching to `hasOwnProperty` would rewrite the callee to `undefined`, so `OBJECT.SUB.toString()` becomes `undefined()` and throws — a regression versus current behavior. Genuinely-absent properties (typos / missing secrets) are not on `Object.prototype`, so `in` already returns `false` for them and the object is not inlined. Accessing a real inherited member inherently requires materializing the object, so `in` does not leak anything beyond what that access already implies. (The `definitions`-map lookup does use `hasOwnProperty`, since config keys are own properties.)

author: alexander-akait

body:
This is consistent with how the plugin already works: every instance processes its own `this.definitions` throughout (`walkDefinitions`, the `expression`/`typeof`/`evaluate` hooks, etc.), and the `hooks.definitions` waterfall is exposed for external observers rather than consumed internally. So an instance that only defines `OBJECT` has never resolved a sibling `OBJECT.SUB2` contributed by a different instance — the member-chain lookup just follows that same per-instance scope. Making member-chain resolution operate on the merged compilation-wide definitions would be a larger, cross-cutting change (the registration side keys handlers per instance too), so I'd prefer to keep it consistent here and treat cross-instance object-key merging as a separate enhancement if it's wanted. Happy to open a follow-up if you'd like it addressed.

## Pull request overview This PR refactors webpack’s internal CSS parsing/tokenization stack by turning `walkCssTokens` into a generator-based tokenizer and embedding a CSS Syntax Level 3–style AST + `parseADeclaration` implementation to support more structured downstream walks (URLs, ICSS exports, etc.), alongside test and snapshot updates to match new spans/behavior. **Changes:** - Refactor `lib/css/walkCssTokens.js` from callback-driven token walking into a token generator, and add a CSS-Syntax-style AST + parsing utilities (incl. `parseADeclaration`). - Update CSS-related tests/helpers to consume the new generator token stream and adjust warning snapshots for updated locations/spans. - Adjust location conversion behavior (`LocConverter`) and update generated typings + spellcheck dictionary entries accordingly. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 10 out of 13 changed files in this pull request and generated 5 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `lib/css/walkCssTokens.js` | Reworked CSS tokenizer into a generator and added AST/parsing utilities used by CSS parsing/walking. | | `lib/util/LocConverter.js` | Tweaks newline retreat logic to improve offset→(line,column) conversion. | | `lib/dependencies/CssIcssExportDependency.js` | Switches identifier escape/unescape helpers to come from `walkCssTokens` exports. | | `test/walkCssTokens.unittest.js` | Updates unit test to iterate token generator and keep snapshot format stable via type mapping. | | `test/helpers/FakeDocument.js` | Updates fake stylesheet parsing to consume generator tokens. | | `test/cssIdentifier.unittest.js` | Adjusts tests to validate identifier escaping utilities directly from `walkCssTokens`. | | `test/configCases/css/**/__snapshots__/warnings.snap` | Updates snapshots for changed warning spans/locations. | | `types.d.ts` | Updates auto-generated TypeScript declarations to reflect refactors/removed typings. | | `cspell.json` | Adds new allowed words introduced by the refactor/docs. |

body: `_isIdentStartCodePointCC` treats non-ASCII as `cc > 0x80`, but CSS Syntax defines non-ASCII as `>= 0x80` (and `isIdentStartCodePoint` above already uses `cc >= 0x80`). This makes U+0080 incorrectly fail the ident-start check and can affect ident-sequence detection. Use `cc >= 0x80` for consistency/spec compliance.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `pushTokenAsNode` closes (pops) the top frame on every `rightParenthesis` token without checking that the current frame is actually a function/`(`. A mismatched `)` inside a `[` or `{` block would incorrectly close the wrong frame and corrupt the AST (the spec treats mismatched closers as normal tokens inside the current block). Only pop when `top.kind` is `"function"` or `"("`; otherwise treat `)` as a leaf token (or keep it in the frame) so recovery stays structurally correct.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `consumeAnAtRule` rejects at-keywords that start with an escape (e.g. `@\66oo`) because it only allows `isIdentStartCodePoint(next)` or `-` after `@`. The tokenizer’s at-keyword logic is based on “would start an ident sequence”, which also allows valid escapes. Consider replacing this guard with `_ifThreeCodePointsWouldStartAnIdentSequence(...)` (using the bytes after `@`) so escaped at-rule names are handled correctly while still rejecting `@ foo`.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The comment says nodes have “no methods other than the `loc` getter”, but `Node` defines `toString()`. Either update the comment to match reality, or drop `toString()` if you want to keep the node surface minimal. If keeping it, consider avoiding direct access to the private `LocConverter#_input` field (store `input` on the node or add an accessor).

## Pull request overview Adds a new `module.parser.html.template` option that transforms HTML module source before dependency extraction, enabling templating languages (Handlebars, EJS, Eta, etc.) to integrate with webpack's HTML module pipeline. The transform runs inside `HtmlModulesPlugin`'s `processResult` hook so parser dependency offsets and the generator's render base stay consistent. **Changes:** - Add `template` option (and `HtmlTemplateContext`) to `HtmlParser`, with helpers for adding file/context/missing/build dependencies and emitting warnings/errors. - Wire `HtmlParser.applyTemplate` into `HtmlModulesPlugin`'s `processResult` so the transformed source becomes the stored module source. - Add schema, generated types, changeset, configCase test, and two examples (`examples/html`, `examples/html-template` with Eta). ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 24 out of 33 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments.

## Pull request overview

Copilot reviewed 8 out of 8 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The inline `<style>` extraction only considers the first text child and ignores any subsequent text nodes. If the tokenizer/AST ever produces multiple text nodes inside `<style>` (e.g., split rawtext), this will truncate the extracted CSS and generate an incomplete dependency. Consider computing the span across all text children (e.g., first text `start` and last text `end`, or concatenating all text node ranges) so the extracted content matches the full element body.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Similar to `<style>`, inline `<script>` extraction only takes the first text child. If the AST contains multiple text nodes in a script body, only the first segment will be bundled into the `data:text/javascript` request. To avoid truncated inline scripts, derive `contentStart/contentEnd` from all text children (or merge/concatenate them).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The `nameEnd` parameter description looks incorrect here: callers pass `node.nameEnd` (end offset of the tag name / right after `<script`), not “end offset of the attribute name”. Updating the JSDoc will prevent confusion for future maintenance.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
This is safe due to the AST invariants. Both <style> and <script> are treated as rawtext elements, so their entire content is consumed as raw text until the corresponding closing tag, without creating intermediate element or comment nodes.

Additionally, appendChild coalesces adjacent text nodes into a single node, ensuring that the body of these elements is always represented as exactly one text child spanning the full content range.

As a result, the case where rawtext content gets split into multiple text nodes cannot occur here, and accessing the content via a single text node is safe.
author: aryanraj45

body: Same invariant as the <style> case: <script> is a rawtext element (buildHtmlAst.js:130), and appendChild merges adjacent text nodes (buildHtmlAst.js:212-221), so the body is always a single text child spanning the whole script. There's no path that produces multiple text segments to truncate.

Adds support for conditionally extracting HTML asset URLs via a per-source `filter` callback, with schema/type updates and a dedicated config case to verify behavior (including cache). **Changes:** - Extend `parser.html.sources[]` entries with an optional `filter(attributes) => boolean` hook. - Wire the new `filter` option through the HTML parser’s internal source table. - Add a new config case + snapshots validating filtered vs unfiltered URL rewriting (including cache variant). ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 8 out of 12 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | types.d.ts | Adds `sources[].filter` typing to the generated global types. | | declarations/WebpackOptions.d.ts | Adds `sources[].filter` typing to the published `Configuration` declarations. | | schemas/WebpackOptions.json | Extends schema to accept a `filter` function for HTML sources entries. | | lib/html/HtmlParser.js | Propagates `entry.filter` into the internal `SourceItem`. | | test/configCases/html/parser-sources-filter/webpack.config.js | New test config exercising `sources[].filter`. | | test/configCases/html/parser-sources-filter/page.html | New HTML fixture containing elements that should / shouldn’t be rewritten. | | test/configCases/html/parser-sources-filter/index.js | Snapshot test asserting conditional extraction behavior. | | test/configCases/html/parser-sources-filter/image.png | New asset fixture used by the test. | | test/configCases/html/parser-sources-filter/__snapshots__/ConfigTest.snap | Expected output snapshot for non-cached run. | | test/configCases/html/parser-sources-filter/__snapshots__/ConfigCacheTest.snap | Expected output snapshot for cached run. |
Files not reviewed (1) * **schemas/plugins/HtmlParserOptions.check.js**: Language not supported
--- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: It would be safer to normalize `entry.filter` to `undefined` unless it is a function (e.g., `typeof entry.filter === \"function\"`). Even though the options schema validates config, this avoids surprising runtime failures if sources entries are constructed programmatically or come from internal defaults/merges that bypass schema validation.

## Pull request overview > [!NOTE] > Copilot was unable to run its full agentic suite in this review. Adds support for asynchronous HTML `template` functions in the HTML parser pipeline by making the template application and the `NormalModule` `processResult` hook async-aware. **Changes:** - Allow `module.parser.html.template` to return a `Promise` in types and implementation. - Convert `NormalModuleCompilationHooks.processResult` from sync to async-waterfall and update call sites. - Add a new config-case test covering async template behavior and warning emission. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 7 out of 9 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | types.d.ts | Updates typings for async HTML template and async `processResult` hook. | | lib/html/HtmlParser.js | Implements Promise support for `template` / `applyTemplate`. | | lib/html/HtmlModulesPlugin.js | Awaits `applyTemplate` via `processResult.tapPromise`. | | lib/NormalModule.js | Migrates `processResult` hook to `AsyncSeriesWaterfallHook` and uses `callAsync`. | | test/configCases/html/parser-template-async/* | Adds a new test case and warning snapshot for async templates. |
--- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The new logic is internally inconsistent: it accepts a synchronous `Buffer` return (`Buffer.isBuffer(transformed)`), but rejects a `Promise<Buffer>` (since the Promise branch only allows `typeof result === "string"`), and the error message/types imply only `string | Promise<string>`. Please align behavior, types, and messaging by either (a) enforcing `string`/`Promise<string>` only (recommended given the option’s purpose is “return the html string to parse”), or (b) fully supporting `Buffer` in both sync and async paths and updating the error message and typedefs accordingly.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Using `instanceof Promise` is brittle (e.g., thenables, Promises from different realms). Prefer checking for a thenable (`transformed && typeof transformed.then === "function"`) or normalizing via `Promise.resolve(transformed)` after excluding valid sync return types; this makes async-template handling more robust.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
## Pull request overview > [!NOTE] > Copilot was unable to run its full agentic suite in this review. Fixes HTML source-attribute handling to align with `html-loader` by skipping values that should not be resolved as modules (whitespace-only, template-like, and absolute URLs), and adds a config-case test to lock in the behavior. **Changes:** - Skip whitespace-only `src`/`srcset` attribute values during source walking. - Skip `${...}`-style template-like attribute values to avoid erroneous module resolution. - Skip `http://`, `https://`, and protocol-relative (`//`) URLs so they pass through unchanged. - Add a new `test/configCases/html/skip-behavior/` snapshot test case. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 6 out of 7 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | test/configCases/html/skip-behavior/webpack.config.js | Adds a minimal config enabling the HTML experiment for the new config-case. | | test/configCases/html/skip-behavior/page.html | Adds HTML fixtures covering whitespace-only, template-like, absolute, and normal relative URLs. | | test/configCases/html/skip-behavior/index.js | Adds a snapshot test asserting the emitted HTML stays as expected. | | test/configCases/html/skip-behavior/__snapshots__/ConfigTest.snap | Records expected HTML output for the non-cache config-case run. | | test/configCases/html/skip-behavior/__snapshots__/ConfigCacheTest.snap | Records expected HTML output for the cache config-case run. | | lib/html/HtmlParser.js | Implements the new skip behavior in the HTML source-walk logic. |
--- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The absolute-URL skip check can be bypassed by leading whitespace (common in `srcset`, e.g. `srcset=\" https://... 2x\"`), since the regex/`startsWith` are applied to the untrimmed `value`. Consider normalizing for the checks (e.g., `const normalized = value.trimStart()` or `trim()`), while still using the original `innerStart/innerEnd` for `sourceStart/sourceEnd` so replacements (when they happen) keep correct offsets.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Using `includes(\"${\")` is a very broad heuristic and will also skip non-template URLs that legitimately contain `${` in the path/query. If the intent is specifically `${...}` template expressions, this would be more robust as a stricter check (e.g., `value.trimStart().startsWith(\"${\")` or a pattern that matches a `${...}` token), which reduces the chance of accidentally disabling processing for valid assets.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: In a hot attribute-walk loop, `trim()` allocates a new string for every attribute value. If this code runs on large HTML documents, consider a non-allocating whitespace test like `!/\\S/.test(attributeValue)` (or equivalent) to detect whitespace-only values without creating a new string. This is optional, but can reduce per-attribute overhead.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
This is a false positive from analyzing the runtime *template* in isolation. `$ensureChunkHandlers$` is a placeholder declared `= undefined` at the top of this file only so the template parses; at code generation it's string-substituted with `__webpack_require__.f` (an always-initialized object), so it is never `undefined` at runtime. The same placeholder is used right above this line (`$ensureChunkHandlers$.$key$Hmr = …`) and throughout the file.

The iterate-and-call pattern here is exactly what `__webpack_require__.e` does internally — `Object.keys(__webpack_require__.f).forEach(key => __webpack_require__.f[key](chunkId, promises))` — which also calls the handler without a `typeof === "function"` guard. Adding such a guard here would be inconsistent with the rest of the runtime and add a dead check on the update path, so I'm leaving it as-is.

body:
In barrel module, we will skip building the target module which isn't side-effect-free too. The behavior is aligned with `rspack`.

For example
```js
// index.js
import { c } from "./barrel.js"

// barrel.js
import { b } from "./b" // We skip building `./b.js`, even if it has side effects.
export * from "./a"
export { c } from "./c"
```

body:
Reviewed the change end-to-end. The core transformation looks correct: `target ||= value` is semantically equivalent to the previous `target = target || value` for every call site here (all targets are member accesses on plain objects/arrays/globals, so the "evaluated twice" / falsy-value concerns don't bite), and the object-literal extraction in the chunk-loading modules preserves the exact emitted formatting. A couple of non-blocking observations, plus two inline notes:

- `lib/library/UmdLibraryPlugin.js` (`accessorAccess`) still emits `${a} = ${a} || {}` for the namespace walk and wasn't migrated to `assignOr`. Is that intentional (UMD aiming for maximal compatibility), or just missed? If intentional, fine — just flagging the inconsistency.

- The browserslist version table and the `node`/`esX` mappings line up with the ES2021 / Chromium-85 baseline, so those look right.

Main suggestion is the missing end-to-end test for the `||=` branch (see inline comment on the unit test).

---
_Generated by [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)_
author: alexander-akait

body:
This unit test covers `assignOr` in isolation, but the seven runtime modules that now call it (jsonp/import-scripts/module/read-file/require/wasm chunk loaders, share + css registry) are only exercised with the default `logicalAssignment: undefined`, i.e. the fallback branch. Nothing asserts that the `||=` branch is actually emitted and produces valid runtime. Consider adding a `configCase` that builds with `output.environment.logicalAssignment: true` and checks the generated runtime contains `||=`, so a future regression in any of those templates is caught end-to-end.

---
_Generated by [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)_
author: alexander-akait

body:
Worth noting in the JSDoc (or just keeping in mind for future callers): `assignOr` only models the `||`/`||=` case, so it must never be used where `target` can hold a legitimate falsy value (`0`, `""`, `false`) — it would overwrite it. All current call sites default to objects/arrays, so this is safe today, but the helper name is generic enough that a later caller could trip on it. A one-line caveat in the doc comment would prevent that.

body: ## Pull request overview Adds first-class Subresource Integrity (SRI) support to webpack’s experimental HTML pipeline via a new `output.html.integrity` option, plus several new HTML integration tests. **Changes:** - Add `output.html.integrity` (boolean/algorithm array/per-asset function) to inject and late-resolve SRI `integrity` attributes for emitted HTML `
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: When `output.html.integrity` is enabled and the authored entry tag already has an `integrity` attribute, this code inserts a second `integrity` attribute instead of replacing/removing the original one. That can produce invalid markup and/or leave the browser using the wrong integrity value for the rewritten chunk URL.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Asset selection for hashing relies on the first `chunk.files` entry that “is/isn't .css”. If `chunk.files` includes non-primary artifacts (e.g. `.js.map`, `.LICENSE.txt`) before the actual JS/CSS file, this can hash the wrong bytes (or fail to find an asset), producing incorrect/missing SRI values. Prefer computing the exact emitted filename via `compilation.getPath(...)` (same logic as URL sentinel resolution) and hash that asset.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
## Pull request overview

Copilot reviewed 12 out of 15 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: When `output.html.integrity` is enabled, the entry tag gets a new `integrity` attribute inserted but an authored `integrity` attribute on the same tag is not removed. That can produce duplicate `integrity` attributes on the entry `<script>`/`<link>` while clones correctly drop the authored value via `dep.integrityRange`. Consider removing the original attribute from the entry tag when `integrityOn` is true, then inserting the sentinel.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `crypto.createHash(algorithm)` will throw for an invalid algorithm name. Right now that exception would bubble out of `processAssets` and fail the compilation with a hard crash. It would be better to catch the error and report it via `compilation.errors` (and skip emitting the integrity attribute for that tag) so users get a clear webpack error message.

The pattern is `https?:`, where the `?` makes the `s` optional, so it matches `http:` *and* `https:` (same as the `http(s)://` noted in the changeset). Deno does support plain `http://` imports too (commonly `http://localhost` in dev), so both are kept external here.

body:
## Off-by-one comparison against length Off-by-one index comparison against length may lead to out-of-bounds read. --- Use a two-phase iteration model instead of <= with a sentinel index:

  • Iterate attributes with a normal bounded loop (i < attrs.length) so index-based access is always in-bounds.
  • Handle the element-body/content case in a separate block after the attribute loop.

    • For this snippet, the best minimal fix is to keep the current single-loop structure but eliminate the off-by-one comparison by iterating over attrs.length + 1 with <:

    • Change line 1433 from:
      • for (let i = 0; i <= attrs.length; i++) {
    • To:
      • for (let i = 0; i < attrs.length + 1; i++) {

    This preserves identical behavior (including the final i === attrs.length content iteration) while removing the direct <= length pattern flagged by CodeQL.
